To resume an existing subagent, Claude uses the `SendMessage` tool with the subagent's agent ID as the `to` field. `SendMessage` is only available when agent teams are enabled via `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`. A stopped subagent that receives a `SendMessage` auto-resumes in the background without a new Agent tool invocation.

#### Plugin subagent restrictions

Plugin subagents do not support `hooks`, `mcpServers`, or `permissionMode` frontmatter fields. These fields are silently ignored when loading from a plugin.

#### Auto-compaction within subagents

Subagents support automatic compaction at approximately 95% context capacity, using the same logic as the main conversation. Compaction events are logged in the subagent's transcript as `{"type": "system", "subtype": "compact_boundary", ...}`.


---

## §7. Context isolation

### Scope

What a subagent inherits from the parent session and what it does not, when invoked through the Agent tool. Covers: which CLAUDE.md files load, which environment variables propagate, whether the subagent sees parent conversation history, whether settings.json applies, whether `.claude/` directory contents (skills, hooks, MCP servers, rules, auto memory) carry over, and how the subagent's context boundary is drawn. Out of scope: agent teams, the `--agent` flag where a subagent runs as the main thread (this section addresses only Agent-tool-invoked subagents).

### Specification

A subagent invoked through the Agent tool runs in its own context window. The boundary is drawn at session-start parity: the subagent receives a fresh system prompt and the same project-discovery surface as a new Claude Code session, but does NOT receive the parent's conversation history, the parent's auto memory, or the parent's preloaded skill content.

What the subagent receives at startup:

| Item | Inherited by Agent-tool subagent | Verbatim source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| System prompt | New, distinct from parent | "Subagents receive only this system prompt (plus basic environment details like working directory), not the full Claude Code system prompt." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| System prompt | Shorter than main session | "The subagent gets its own system prompt, shorter than the main session's. For the general-purpose agent, it's a brief prompt plus environment details." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Working directory | Inherited | "A subagent starts in the main conversation's current working directory." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| `cd` persistence | Not inherited; isolated | "Within a subagent, `cd` commands do not persist between Bash or PowerShell tool calls and do not affect the main conversation's working directory." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Project CLAUDE.md | Loaded as own copy | "The subagent loads CLAUDE.md too. Same file, same content, but it counts against the subagent's context, not yours. The built-in Explore and Plan agents skip this for a smaller context." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Project CLAUDE.md (general note) | Loaded with same MCP and skill setup | "Claude delegates the research to a subagent with a fresh, separate context window. It loads CLAUDE.md and the same MCP and skill setup, but starts without your conversation history or the main session's auto memory." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Parent conversation history | NOT inherited | "starts without your conversation history or the main session's auto memory." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Parent auto memory (`MEMORY.md`) | NOT inherited | "The main session's auto memory is not included. If a custom agent has memory: in its frontmatter, it loads its own separate MEMORY.md here instead." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Parent auto memory (`MEMORY.md`) | NOT inherited (corroborated) | "This is distinct from your main session auto memory at `~/.claude/projects/`: each subagent reads and writes its own MEMORY.md, not yours." [claude-directory](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-directory) |
| Subagent's own auto memory | Loaded only if `memory:` frontmatter set | "If a custom agent has memory: in its frontmatter, it loads its own separate MEMORY.md here instead." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Subagent's own auto memory loading | First 200 lines / 25KB into system prompt | "The subagent's system prompt also includes the first 200 lines or 25KB of `MEMORY.md` in the memory directory, whichever comes first" [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Skills (parent's loaded skill content) | NOT inherited | "Subagents don't inherit skills from the parent conversation; you must list them explicitly." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Skills (`skills:` frontmatter) | Full content injected at startup | "The full content of each skill is injected into the subagent's context, not just made available for invocation." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| MCP servers | Inherited by default | "By default, subagents inherit all tools from the main conversation, including MCP tools." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| MCP servers (general) | Same MCP setup as parent | "It loads CLAUDE.md and the same MCP and skill setup" [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| MCP scoping (inline `mcpServers:`) | Subagent-only inline servers | "Inline servers defined here are connected when the subagent starts and disconnected when it finishes." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Tools (general) | Inherited unless restricted | "Inherits all tools if omitted" [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Tools (specific exclusions) | Several parent tools unavailable in nested context | "It gets most of the parent's tools, minus several that don't apply in a nested context, including plan-mode controls, background-task tools, and by default the Agent tool itself to prevent recursion." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Permission context | Inherited; mode can be overridden subject to parent precedence | "Subagents inherit the permission context from the main conversation and can override the mode, except when the parent mode takes precedence as described below." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Permission mode (parent `bypassPermissions` / `acceptEdits`) | Forced, cannot override | "If the parent uses `bypassPermissions` or `acceptEdits`, this takes precedence and cannot be overridden." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Permission mode (parent auto mode) | Inherited; subagent's `permissionMode` ignored | "If the parent uses auto mode, the subagent inherits auto mode and any `permissionMode` in its frontmatter is ignored: the classifier evaluates the subagent's tool calls with the same block and allow rules as the parent session." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Hooks (frontmatter on subagent) | Run only while subagent active | "Define hooks directly in the subagent's markdown file. These hooks only run while that specific subagent is active and are cleaned up when it finishes." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Hooks (frontmatter `Stop`) | Converted to `SubagentStop` at runtime | "`Stop` hooks in frontmatter are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` events." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| `--agents` JSON definition | Session-scoped subagent | "They exist only for that session and aren't saved to disk" [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Plugin subagent restrictions | `hooks`, `mcpServers`, `permissionMode` ignored | "For security reasons, plugin subagents do not support the `hooks`, `mcpServers`, or `permissionMode` frontmatter fields. These fields are ignored when loading agents from a plugin." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Built-in Explore and Plan | Skip CLAUDE.md load | "The built-in Explore and Plan agents skip this for a smaller context." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Auto-compaction | Same logic as main conversation | "Subagents support automatic compaction using the same logic as the main conversation." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| Subagent task prompt | Written by Claude, not user prompt | "Instead of a user prompt, the subagent receives the task Claude wrote for it" [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| Settings.json (parent) | Primary sources do not specify whether `settings.json` keys re-apply inside the subagent's context (see Appendix B) | — |
| Environment variables (general OS env) | Primary sources do not specify general-OS-env propagation through the Agent tool boundary | — |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` env | Influences subagent model resolution | "The `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` environment variable, if set" [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) |
| `CLAUDE_AUTOCOMPACT_PCT_OVERRIDE` env | Applies to both main and subagents | "Applies to both main conversations and subagents." [env-vars](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars) |
| `TASK_MAX_OUTPUT_LENGTH` env | Bounds subagent output | "Maximum number of characters in subagent output before truncation" [env-vars](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars) |
| `CLAUDE_AGENT_SDK_DISABLE_BUILTIN_AGENTS` | Disables built-in subagent types in `-p` mode | "Set to `1` to disable all built-in subagent types such as Explore and Plan. Only applies in non-interactive mode" [env-vars](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars) |
| `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS` | Disables `run_in_background` on subagent tools | "disable all background task functionality, including the `run_in_background` parameter on Bash and subagent tools" [env-vars](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars) |
| `.claude/` discovery (project agents, project skills, project rules) | Available to subagent through normal session-start discovery, modulated by per-subagent fields | "It loads CLAUDE.md and the same MCP and skill setup" [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window) |
| `--add-dir` directories' CLAUDE.md | Not loaded unless `CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1` (general session rule) | "By default, CLAUDE.md files from these directories are not loaded." [memory](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory) |

### Behavior

The subagent boots like a fresh session, then runs against a task prompt Claude writes for it. Its context contains: a shorter system prompt, environment details, the project CLAUDE.md (unless built-in Explore/Plan, which skip it), the same MCP and skill setup as the parent, the task prompt, and any `skills:` content explicitly listed in frontmatter. It does NOT contain: the parent's conversation history, the parent's auto memory, or the parent's preloaded skill content.

A subagent with `memory:` frontmatter loads its own separate `MEMORY.md` (first 200 lines or 25KB) into its system prompt; this is a different file from the main session's auto memory.

Tool inheritance: by default, all parent tools are inherited, including MCP tools. Several tools are removed: plan-mode controls, background-task tools, and by default the Agent tool (preventing recursion). Restrictions are applied via `tools` (allowlist) and `disallowedTools` (denylist). When both are set, `disallowedTools` is applied first, then `tools` is resolved against the remaining pool.

Permission inheritance: the subagent inherits the parent's permission context. Parent `bypassPermissions` or `acceptEdits` cannot be overridden by the subagent. Parent `auto` mode forces the subagent into auto mode regardless of frontmatter.

Working directory: inherited from the main conversation. `cd` commands inside the subagent do not persist between tool calls and do not affect the main conversation's working directory. To give the subagent an isolated checkout, set `isolation: worktree`.

Hooks: hooks defined in subagent frontmatter run only while that subagent is active and are cleaned up when it finishes. Frontmatter `Stop` hooks become `SubagentStop` events. Hooks defined in `settings.json` for `SubagentStart` and `SubagentStop` fire in the main session when subagents begin or complete. Plugin-sourced subagents have their `hooks`, `mcpServers`, and `permissionMode` frontmatter fields ignored.

Compaction: subagent transcripts persist independently of the main conversation. Main-conversation compaction does not affect subagent transcripts; they are stored in separate files at `~/.claude/projects/{project}/{sessionId}/subagents/agent-{agentId}.jsonl`. Subagent auto-compaction uses the same logic as the main conversation.

### Edge cases

- Built-in Explore and Plan subagents skip the CLAUDE.md load step that custom and general-purpose subagents perform. This produces a smaller context but means these built-ins do not pick up project conventions encoded in CLAUDE.md.
- A subagent invoked via @-mention guarantees the named subagent runs; natural-language naming leaves the choice to Claude.
- The Agent tool is unavailable to subagents by default. Sub-agents.md states: "Subagents cannot spawn other subagents, so `Agent(agent_type)` has no effect in subagent definitions." Recursion is blocked at the platform level.
- `--agents` CLI subagents exist only for the current session and are not saved to disk. They use the same frontmatter fields as file-based subagents.
- Plugin subagents silently drop `hooks`, `mcpServers`, and `permissionMode` frontmatter; the surrounding session's permission rules apply instead.
- Inline `mcpServers:` defined in subagent frontmatter connect at subagent start and disconnect at finish. String references reuse parent connections.
- Setting `isolation: worktree` gives the subagent a temporary git worktree (an isolated copy of the repository), with automatic cleanup if no changes are made.
- `--add-dir` directories grant file access only and are not scanned for subagents; CLAUDE.md from those directories does not load by default in any session.
- The `claudeMdExcludes` setting can skip ancestor CLAUDE.md files. Whether these exclusions apply identically inside a subagent's CLAUDE.md load is not stated in the primary sources.
- A subagent can be resumed via `SendMessage` (gated behind `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`); resumed subagents retain their full conversation history, including all previous tool calls, results, and reasoning.
- The `CLAUDECODE` environment variable is set to `1` in shell environments Claude Code spawns. Whether subagent-spawned Bash inherits this from the parent or is independently set is not addressed.


---

## §8. Output routing

### Scope

How a subagent's output reaches the orchestrator: the shape of the return value, what the orchestrator sees in its transcript when the subagent completes, how token usage and context flow back, what the orchestrator observes on subagent error / cancellation / API failure, and which hook events fire along the return path.

In scope: subagents spawned through the Agent tool from a parent Claude Code session (the Agent tool was renamed from Task in v2.1.63). Out of scope: agent teams (separate sessions, governed by `SendMessage` / `TeammateIdle`), `--agent` main-session mode.

### Specification

#### Shape of the value returned to the Agent tool caller

Primary sources do not name a single normative "return value schema" for the Agent tool. They describe the return as a **summary**, and they specify a `last_assistant_message` field carried on the `SubagentStop` hook payload that holds the subagent's final assistant text:

> "the subagent does that work in its own context and returns only the summary." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

> "When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent's description, it delegates to that subagent, which works independently and returns results." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

> "When done, they return a summary. This isolation is why subagents help with long sessions." [how-claude-code-works §The context window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works#the-context-window)

> "The `last_assistant_message` field contains the text content of the subagent's final response, so hooks can access it without parsing the transcript file." [hooks §SubagentStop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#subagentstop)

> "When a subagent completes, Claude receives its agent ID." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

The return surface that primary sources document explicitly is therefore: (a) the subagent's final assistant message text, and (b) an agent ID by which the parent can later address the subagent (via `SendMessage`, gated on `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`).

#### What appears in the orchestrator's transcript

The orchestrator session and the subagent keep **separate transcript files**. The subagent's transcript is not folded into the parent's transcript on completion:

> "Subagent transcripts persist independently of the main conversation: ... They're stored in separate files." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

> "The `transcript_path` is the main session's transcript, while `agent_transcript_path` is the subagent's own transcript stored in a nested `subagents/` folder." [hooks §SubagentStop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#subagentstop)

> "find IDs in the transcript files at `~/.claude/projects/{project}/{sessionId}/subagents/`. Each transcript is stored as `agent-{agentId}.jsonl`." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

What primary sources state the orchestrator receives on completion is the **summary** (above) and the **agent ID** (above). They do not state that the subagent's tool calls, intermediate reasoning, or full transcript body are inserted into the orchestrator's transcript.

#### Token budget for the response

Primary sources do not state a fixed token budget on the value returned from a subagent to the orchestrator. They state two related budgets:

1. The subagent has its own context window and auto-compacts it independently:

   > "Subagents support automatic compaction using the same logic as the main conversation. By default, auto-compaction triggers at approximately 95% capacity." [sub-agents §Common patterns](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#common-patterns)

2. The subagent's `maxTurns` frontmatter caps agentic turns inside the subagent.

Hook output that is injected into context is capped at 10,000 characters (this governs hook payloads, not the subagent return value itself):

> "Hook output injected into context (`additionalContext`, `systemMessage`, or plain stdout) is capped at 10,000 characters. Output that exceeds this limit is saved to a file and replaced with a preview and file path, the same way large tool results are handled." [hooks §JSON output](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#json-output)

#### How token usage flows back

Primary sources state that the subagent's intermediate context **does not bloat the parent context**:

> "[Subagents](/en/sub-agents) get their own fresh context, completely separate from your main conversation. Their work doesn't bloat your context." [how-claude-code-works §The context window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works#the-context-window)

> "**Preserve context** by keeping exploration and implementation out of your main conversation" [sub-agents §Define hooks for subagents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#define-hooks-for-subagents)

The cost back to the parent is the returned summary itself, which the parent must absorb:

> "When subagents complete, their results return to your main conversation. Running many subagents that each return detailed results can consume significant context." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

Primary sources do not specify token-accounting / billing telemetry surfaced to the orchestrator on subagent return.

#### Hook events on the return path

The hook events that fire on subagent completion, ordered:

| Event           | When it fires                                                     | Source     |
| :-------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------- | :----------|
| `SubagentStop`  | "When a subagent finishes"                                        | hooks.md   |
| `SubagentStop`  | "Runs when a Claude Code subagent has finished responding."       | hooks.md   |
| `WorktreeRemove`| "fires when a worktree is being removed ... when a subagent with `isolation: \"worktree\"` finishes" | hooks.md |

A subagent's frontmatter `Stop` hook is **rewritten to `SubagentStop` at runtime**:

> "`Stop` hooks in frontmatter are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` events." [sub-agents §Run subagents in foreground or](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#run-subagents-in-foreground-or-background)

> "For subagents, `Stop` hooks are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` since that is the event that fires when a subagent completes." [hooks §Hooks in skills and agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#hooks-in-skills-and-agents)

Two additional events bracket the subagent lifecycle on the spawn side:

| Event           | When it fires                                                     |
| :-------------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `SubagentStart` | "When a subagent is spawned" / "Runs when a Claude Code subagent is spawned via the Agent tool." |
| `WorktreeCreate`| Fires "when a subagent uses `isolation: \"worktree\"`"            |

#### `SubagentStop` payload

> "SubagentStop hooks receive `stop_hook_active`, `agent_id`, `agent_type`, `agent_transcript_path`, and `last_assistant_message`." [hooks §SubagentStop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#subagentstop)

Example payload:

```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123.jsonl",
  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  "permission_mode": "default",
  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStop",
  "stop_hook_active": false,
  "agent_id": "def456",
  "agent_type": "Explore",
  "agent_transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123/subagents/agent-def456.jsonl",
  "last_assistant_message": "Analysis complete. Found 3 potential issues..."
}
```

#### `SubagentStop` decision control

> "SubagentStop hooks use the same decision control format as Stop hooks." [hooks §SubagentStop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#subagentstop)

| Field      | Description                                                                |
| :--------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `decision` | `"block"` prevents Claude from stopping. Omit to allow Claude to stop      |
| `reason`   | Required when `decision` is `"block"`. Tells Claude why it should continue |

Exit-code behavior:

> "`SubagentStop` | Yes | Prevents the subagent from stopping" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

#### Error / failure surfacing

Primary sources document four failure modes on the return path.

**1. API error during the subagent's turn.** `StopFailure` is documented as the API-error counterpart of `Stop`:

> "Runs instead of [Stop](#stop) when the turn ends due to an API error. Output and exit code are ignored." [hooks §StopFailure](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#stopfailure)

Matcher values: `rate_limit`, `authentication_failed`, `billing_error`, `invalid_request`, `server_error`, `max_output_tokens`, `unknown`. Primary sources do **not** name a `SubagentStopFailure` event; the hook-events table lists `StopFailure` only.

**2. Background-subagent permission failure.**

> "Background subagents run concurrently while you continue working. Before launching, Claude Code prompts for any tool permissions the subagent will need ... Once running, the subagent inherits these permissions and auto-denies anything not pre-approved. If a background subagent needs to ask clarifying questions, that tool call fails but the subagent continues." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

> "If a background subagent fails due to missing permissions, you can start a new foreground subagent with the same task to retry with interactive prompts." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

**3. User interrupt of the parent.** `Stop` does not fire on user interrupt. Primary sources do not state whether `SubagentStop` fires when the parent session is interrupted while a subagent is running.

**4. `maxTurns` exhaustion.** Primary sources do not specify the surfaced shape (error, partial result, or both) when `maxTurns` is reached.

#### Resumption after return

> "When a subagent completes, Claude receives its agent ID. Claude uses the `SendMessage` tool with the agent's ID as the `to` field to resume it. The `SendMessage` tool is only available when [agent teams](/en/agent-teams) are enabled via `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

> "If a stopped subagent receives a `SendMessage`, it auto-resumes in the background without requiring a new `Agent` invocation." [sub-agents §Choose the subagent scope](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-the-subagent-scope)

> "Resumed subagents retain their full conversation history, including all previous tool calls, results, and reasoning. The subagent picks up exactly where it stopped rather than starting fresh." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

### Behavior

- The orchestrator's transcript and the subagent's transcript are separate JSONL files. The subagent's lives at `~/.claude/projects/{project}/{sessionId}/subagents/agent-{agentId}.jsonl`. On return the orchestrator receives a summary and the subagent's agent ID; the subagent's tool calls and reasoning remain in the subagent transcript.
- The subagent runs its own context window and compacts it independently at ~95% by default. Compaction events appear as `compact_boundary` records in the subagent transcript.
- `SubagentStop` is the canonical return-path hook. A frontmatter `Stop` written for a subagent definition is rewritten to `SubagentStop` at runtime.
- `SubagentStop` exposes the final assistant text as `last_assistant_message` so the hook avoids parsing the transcript file.
- `SubagentStop` exit code 2 keeps the subagent running. Returned JSON `decision: "block"` with a `reason` instructs the subagent to continue.
- For `isolation: worktree` subagents, `WorktreeRemove` fires after the subagent finishes. The worktree is auto-cleaned only if the subagent made no changes.
- The `SubagentStop` payload identifies the subagent by `agent_id` and `agent_type`, allowing matcher-based routing.
- When the orchestrator runs `--agent <name>` itself, hooks executed inside the subagent receive `agent_id` and `agent_type` in the common input fields, so a hook can distinguish subagent-scope from main-thread-scope calls.

### Edge cases

- **Frontmatter `Stop` semantics flip.** A user who writes `Stop:` in subagent frontmatter expecting it to fire when the *parent* main session stops will instead get `SubagentStop`. Frontmatter hooks "fire when the agent is spawned as a subagent through the Agent tool or an @-mention. They do not fire when the agent runs as the main session via `--agent` or the `agent` setting" [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents).
- **Re-entrant `SubagentStop` blocking.** `SubagentStop` exit 2 prevents the subagent from stopping. Combined with `stop_hook_active` (carried in the payload), a hook must self-check or it loops indefinitely. Primary sources do not state a built-in retry cap.
- **Auto-mode classifier denials inside a subagent.** If the parent uses `auto` permission mode, the subagent inherits it and the per-call classifier evaluates each tool call. Denials surface as `PermissionDenied` events; `PermissionDenied` exit code and stderr are ignored. Primary sources do not state how a sequence of classifier denials is surfaced to the orchestrator on subagent completion.
- **Compaction inside a subagent.** Auto-compaction can stop with a "thrashing error" when a single tool result keeps refilling the context. Primary sources do not specify how this surfaces in the value returned to the orchestrator.
- **Result-overflow into orchestrator context.** Multiple parallel subagents that each return detailed results can themselves push the orchestrator over its budget.
- **Agent ID lifecycle.** The agent ID returned to the orchestrator is the same one used by `SendMessage` to resume, but `SendMessage` only exists with the agent-teams flag set. Without the flag, primary sources do not state whether the orchestrator can address the agent ID at all.
- **`bypassPermissions` cannot be reduced by a subagent.** Anything the subagent does is performed under the parent's elevated permission posture, and that elevation is invisibly carried into whatever it returns.
- **Plugin subagents are silently restricted.** A plugin-distributed subagent that *appears* to define a `SubagentStop` hook in its frontmatter has no return-path hook installed.


---

## §9. Permissions, sandbox, IAM

### Scope

The Claude Code platform's permission rule syntax, permission modes, sandbox boundaries, and authentication scope as they apply to subagent invocations.

### Specification

#### Permission rule syntax

Rules follow the format `Tool` or `Tool(specifier)`.

**Match-all form** (no parentheses or bare wildcard):

| Rule | Effect |
|---|---|
| `Bash` | All Bash commands |
| `WebFetch` | All web fetch requests |
| `Read` | All file reads |

`Bash(*)` is equivalent to `Bash`.

**Specifier form:**

| Rule | Effect |
|---|---|
| `Bash(npm run build)` | Exact command match |
| `Read(./.env)` | Exact file path match |
| `WebFetch(domain:example.com)` | All fetches to that domain |

**Wildcard patterns (Bash only):**

`*` matches any character sequence including spaces. Position matters:

- `Bash(npm run *)` — commands starting with `npm run`
- `Bash(* install)` — commands ending with ` install`
- `Bash(git * main)` — commands like `git checkout main`, `git push origin main`
- `Bash(ls *)` — matches `ls -la` but NOT `lsof` (space enforces word boundary)
- `Bash(ls*)` — matches both `ls -la` and `lsof`
- `Bash(ls:*)` — equivalent to `Bash(ls *)` (`:*` suffix only recognized at end)

A single `*` spans multiple arguments: `Bash(git:*)` matches `git log --oneline --all`.

**Compound commands:** Claude Code recognizes shell operators (`&&`, `||`, `;`, `|`, `|&`, `&`, newlines). A rule must match each subcommand independently — `Bash(safe-cmd *)` does NOT authorize `safe-cmd && other-cmd`.

**Process wrappers stripped before matching:** `timeout`, `time`, `nice`, `nohup`, `stdbuf`, and bare `xargs`. So `Bash(npm test *)` also matches `timeout 30 npm test`. Development environment runners (`direnv exec`, `devbox run`, `mise exec`, `npx`, `docker exec`) are NOT stripped.

**Read-only built-in commands** (run without a permission prompt in every mode): `ls`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `grep`, `find`, `wc`, `diff`, `stat`, `du`, `cd`, and read-only forms of `git`. The set is not configurable.

**Read and Edit path pattern types:**

| Pattern | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `//path` | Absolute from filesystem root | `Read(//Users/alice/secrets/**)` |
| `~/path` | From home directory | `Read(~/Documents/*.pdf)` |
| `/path` | Relative to project root | `Edit(/src/**/*.ts)` |
| `path` or `./path` | Relative to cwd | `Read(*.env)` |

`/Users/alice/file` is NOT an absolute path — it is relative to the project root. Use `//Users/alice/file` for absolute paths. `*` matches within one directory; `**` matches recursively.

**Edit rules** apply to all built-in file-editing tools. `Read` rules apply to all built-in file-reading tools (Grep, Glob). Neither applies to Bash subprocesses — `Read(./.env)` deny blocks the Read tool but does NOT prevent `cat .env` in Bash.

**Symlink handling:**
- Allow rules: apply only when both the symlink path AND its target match. Symlink inside an allowed directory pointing outside still prompts.
- Deny rules: apply when EITHER the symlink path or its target matches.

**MCP rules:**

| Rule | Effect |
|---|---|
| `mcp__puppeteer` | All tools from the `puppeteer` server |
| `mcp__puppeteer__*` | Same (wildcard form) |
| `mcp__puppeteer__puppeteer_navigate` | Specific tool |

**Agent (subagent) rules:**

Use `Agent(AgentName)` to control which subagents Claude can spawn:

- `Agent(Explore)` — matches the Explore subagent
- `Agent(my-custom-agent)` — matches a custom subagent

Add to the `deny` array or use `--disallowedTools` to disable a subagent.

**Rule evaluation order:** deny → ask → allow. The first matching rule wins. Deny rules always take precedence.

**Settings precedence (highest to lowest):**
1. Managed settings (cannot be overridden, including by CLI arguments)
2. Command-line arguments (temporary session overrides)
3. Local project settings (`.claude/settings.local.json`)
4. Shared project settings (`.claude/settings.json`)
5. User settings (`~/.claude/settings.json`)

If a tool is denied at any level, no other level can allow it.

**Hooks and permission interaction:**
- PreToolUse hook output cannot bypass deny or ask rules. A matching deny rule blocks the call even if the hook returned `"allow"`.
- A blocking hook (exit code 2) stops the call before permission rules are evaluated, overriding even an allow rule.

#### Permission modes

Set via `permissions.defaultMode` in settings, `--permission-mode <mode>` at startup, or `Shift+Tab` to cycle during a session.

| Mode | What runs without asking | Key behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `default` | Reads only | Prompts for every tool on first use |
| `acceptEdits` | Reads, file edits in working directory, `mkdir`/`touch`/`rm`/`rmdir`/`mv`/`cp`/`sed` | Paths outside working directory or protected paths still prompt |
| `plan` | Reads only | Claude reads and proposes but does not edit or execute commands |
| `auto` | Everything, with background classifier checks | Research preview; classifier blocks escalating or exfiltration actions |
| `dontAsk` | Only `permissions.allow`-listed tools and read-only Bash commands | All else auto-denied; explicit `ask` rules are denied rather than prompting |
| `bypassPermissions` | Everything except protected paths | No prompts, no safety checks; protected-path writes still prompt |

Modes set the baseline; permission rules layer on top. Exception: `bypassPermissions` skips the permission layer entirely.

**acceptEdits auto-approved filesystem commands** are also auto-approved when prefixed with safe env vars (`LANG=C`, `NO_COLOR=1`) or process wrappers (`timeout`, `nice`, `nohup`). Auto-approval applies only to paths inside the working directory or `additionalDirectories`.

**plan mode:** reads files and runs shell commands to explore, writes a plan, but does not edit source. Permission prompts still apply as in default mode.

**auto mode requirements:**
- Claude Code v2.1.83 or later
- Plan: Max, Team, Enterprise, or API (not Pro)
- Team/Enterprise: admin must enable it in admin settings
- Model: Claude Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.6, or Opus 4.7 on Team/Enterprise/API; Opus 4.7 only on Max
- Provider: Anthropic API only (not Bedrock, Vertex, or Foundry)

**auto mode classifier decision order:**
1. Actions matching allow or deny rules resolve immediately.
2. Read-only actions and file edits in working directory (except protected paths) are auto-approved.
3. Everything else goes to the classifier.
4. If classifier blocks, Claude receives the reason and tries an alternative.

**auto mode: what is blocked by default:**
- `curl | bash` and downloading+executing code
- Sending sensitive data to external endpoints
- Production deploys and migrations
- Mass deletion on cloud storage
- Granting IAM or repo permissions
- Modifying shared infrastructure
- Irreversibly destroying files that existed before the session
- Force push or pushing directly to `main`

**auto mode: what is allowed by default:**
- Local file operations in working directory
- Installing dependencies from lock files or manifests
- Reading `.env` and sending credentials to their matching API
- Read-only HTTP requests
- Pushing to the branch the session started on or one Claude created

**auto mode fallback:** If classifier blocks 3 consecutive actions or 20 total, auto mode pauses and Claude Code resumes prompting. Thresholds are not configurable.

**bypassPermissions:** Writes to `.git`, `.claude`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, `.husky` still prompt. Writes to `.claude/commands`, `.claude/agents`, `.claude/skills` do NOT prompt. Administrators can disable this mode with `permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode: "disable"` in managed settings.

**dontAsk in non-interactive mode (`-p`):** Repeated classifier blocks abort the session since no user is present to prompt.

**Protected paths — never auto-approved in any mode:**

Directories: `.git`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, `.husky`, `.claude` (except `.claude/commands`, `.claude/agents`, `.claude/skills`, `.claude/worktrees`)

Files: `.gitconfig`, `.gitmodules`, `.bashrc`, `.bash_profile`, `.zshrc`, `.zprofile`, `.profile`, `.ripgreprc`, `.mcp.json`, `.claude.json`

In `default`, `acceptEdits`, `plan`, `bypassPermissions`: protected-path writes prompt. In `auto`: route to classifier. In `dontAsk`: denied.

#### auto mode and subagents (verbatim from source)

The auto mode classifier applies to subagent invocations at three explicit checkpoints:

> "1. Before a subagent starts, the delegated task description is evaluated, so a dangerous-looking task is blocked at spawn time. 2. While the subagent runs, each of its actions goes through the classifier with the same rules as the parent session, and any `permissionMode` in the subagent's frontmatter is ignored. 3. When the subagent finishes, the classifier reviews its full action history; if that return check flags a concern, a security warning is prepended to the subagent's results." [permission-modes §When auto mode falls back](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes#when-auto-mode-falls-back)

Key fact: a subagent's frontmatter `permissionMode` field is ignored when auto mode is active.

On entering auto mode, broad allow rules are dropped:
- Blanket `Bash(*)`
- Wildcarded interpreters like `Bash(python*)`
- Package-manager run commands
- `Agent` allow rules

Narrow rules like `Bash(npm test)` carry over. Dropped rules are restored when auto mode is exited.

#### Sandbox boundaries

Sandboxing applies only to Bash commands and their child processes. Built-in file tools (Read, Edit, Write) use the permission system, not the sandbox.

**OS-level enforcement:**
- macOS: Seatbelt
- Linux: bubblewrap
- WSL2: bubblewrap
- WSL1: not supported

All child processes spawned by sandboxed commands inherit sandbox restrictions.

**Default filesystem behavior:**
- Write access: current working directory and subdirectories only
- Read access: entire filesystem except denied directories
- Blocked: modifications outside working directory without explicit permission

**Configurable via `sandbox.filesystem`:**
- `allowWrite`: grant subprocess write access to additional paths
- `denyWrite`: block subprocess write access
- `denyRead`: block subprocess read access
- `allowRead`: re-allow reading within a denied region (takes precedence over `denyRead`)

When `sandbox.filesystem.*` arrays are defined in multiple settings scopes, they merge — paths from all scopes combine, they are not replaced.

**Path prefix resolution in sandbox settings** (differs from Read/Edit permission rules):

| Prefix | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `/` | Absolute path from filesystem root |
| `~/` | Relative to home directory |
| `./` or no prefix | Relative to project root (project settings) or `~/.claude` (user settings) |

**Network isolation:** A proxy server running outside the sandbox controls domain access. New domain requests trigger permission prompts (unless `allowManagedDomainsOnly` blocks them automatically). Restrictions apply to all scripts, programs, and subprocesses.

**Sandbox modes:**

- **Auto-allow mode (`autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`, default when sandbox enabled):** Bash commands inside the sandbox are automatically allowed without permission prompts. Commands that cannot be sandboxed fall back to the regular permission flow. Explicit deny rules are always respected. Ask rules apply only to fallback commands.
- **Regular permissions mode:** All Bash commands go through the standard permission flow even when sandboxed.

> "Auto-allow mode works independently of your permission mode setting. Even if you're not in 'accept edits' mode, sandboxed bash commands will run automatically when auto-allow is enabled. This means bash commands that modify files within the sandbox boundaries will execute without prompting, even when file edit tools would normally require approval." [sandboxing §Sandbox modes](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing#sandbox-modes)

When sandboxing is enabled with `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`, sandboxed Bash commands run without prompting even if `permissions` includes `ask: Bash(*)`. The sandbox boundary substitutes for the per-command prompt.

**Escape hatch:** When a command fails due to sandbox restrictions, Claude may retry it with the `dangerouslyDisableSandbox` parameter. Such commands go through the normal permission flow. Disable with `"allowUnsandboxedCommands": false` in sandbox settings.

**Sandbox does NOT cover:**
- Built-in file tools (Read, Edit, Write) — these use the permission system
- Computer use (runs on actual desktop, gated by per-app permission prompts)

**Sandbox + permissions interaction:**
- `Read`/`Edit` deny rules from permissions are merged into the sandbox filesystem configuration.
- `sandbox.filesystem.denyWrite`/`denyRead` paths are merged with `Edit(...)`/`Read(...)` permission deny rules.
- Permission deny rules block Claude from attempting access; sandbox restrictions enforce at OS level even if a prompt injection bypasses Claude's decision-making.

#### Managed-only settings relevant to permissions/sandbox

These settings are read only from managed settings (placing them in user or project settings has no effect):

| Setting | Effect |
|---|---|
| `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly` | Prevents user and project settings from defining allow/ask/deny rules. Only managed rules apply. |
| `allowManagedHooksOnly` | Only managed hooks, SDK hooks, and force-enabled plugin hooks are loaded. User, project, and other plugin hooks are blocked. |
| `allowManagedMcpServersOnly` | Only `allowedMcpServers` from managed settings apply. |
| `sandbox.filesystem.allowManagedReadPathsOnly` | Only managed `allowRead` paths respected. User/project `allowRead` ignored; `denyRead` still merges from all sources. |
| `sandbox.network.allowManagedDomainsOnly` | Only managed `allowedDomains` and `WebFetch(domain:...)` allow rules apply. Non-allowed domains blocked automatically without prompting. |

`permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode` and `permissions.disableAutoMode` can be set at any scope but are most effective in managed settings (cannot be overridden).

#### Authentication scope for subagents

Authentication is not subagent-specific in the source documentation. Sources describe authentication at the session/process level:

**Credential precedence (highest to lowest):**
1. Cloud provider credentials (`CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK`, `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX`, `CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY`)
2. `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` environment variable
3. `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable
4. `apiKeyHelper` script output
5. `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` environment variable
6. Subscription OAuth credentials from `/login`

`apiKeyHelper`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, and `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` apply to terminal CLI sessions only. Claude Desktop and remote sessions use OAuth exclusively.

No source explicitly states whether subagents inherit the parent's credentials or establish their own. The authentication system operates at the process level; the sources are silent on per-subagent credential scope.

### Behavior

**Rule evaluation is deterministic:** deny → ask → allow, first match wins. A tool denied in managed settings cannot be unlocked at any other level.

**Hooks interact with rules in a specific order:**
- PreToolUse hook runs before the permission prompt.
- A blocking hook (exit code 2) stops execution before rules are evaluated — overrides even an allow rule.
- A permissive hook result does NOT override deny or ask rules.

**auto mode drops broad allow rules at entry** (including `Agent` allow rules) and restores them at exit.

**auto mode classifier sees user messages, tool calls, and CLAUDE.md content.** Tool results are stripped — hostile content in a file cannot manipulate the classifier directly.

**auto mode boundaries stated in conversation** are enforced by the classifier re-reading the transcript on each check. They can be lost if context compaction removes the originating message.

**Sandbox auto-allow overrides the permission mode for sandboxed Bash:** `ask: Bash(*)` is bypassed for sandboxed commands when `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`.

**`autoMode.allow` and `soft_deny` replace the entire default list** for that section if set. Setting `soft_deny` with one entry discards all built-in block rules.

**autoMode classifier reads from user settings, `.claude/settings.local.json`, and managed settings only.** It does NOT read from shared project settings (`.claude/settings.json`) — a checked-in repo could otherwise inject its own allow rules.

**`autoMode.allow` allows developers to override `soft_deny` from managed settings** — the combination is additive, not a hard policy boundary. For rules that must hold, use `permissions.deny` in managed settings.

### Edge cases

**`bypassPermissions` and `.claude/agents` writes:** Despite `.claude` being a protected directory, writes to `.claude/commands`, `.claude/agents`, and `.claude/skills` are explicitly exempt from prompting in all modes, including `bypassPermissions`. Claude routinely writes there when creating skills, subagents, and commands.

**`acceptEdits` filesystem commands with process wrappers:** `mkdir`, `touch`, `rm`, `rmdir`, `mv`, `cp`, `sed` are auto-approved when prefixed with safe env vars or wrappers, but only for paths inside the working directory or `additionalDirectories`. Paths outside scope still prompt.

**Compound command rule saving:** Approving `git status && npm test` with "Yes, don't ask again" saves a rule for `npm test` (not the compound string). Up to 5 rules may be saved for a single compound command.

**`cd` inside working directory** is read-only. `cd packages/api && ls` runs without a prompt when each part qualifies. Combining `cd` with `git` in one compound command always prompts.

**Bash constraint patterns are fragile:** `Bash(curl http://github.com/ *)` does NOT match `curl -X GET http://github.com/...`, `curl https://github.com/...`, or `URL=http://github.com && curl $URL`. URL filtering requires WebFetch permission rules or PreToolUse hooks, not Bash patterns.

**`Read`/`Edit` deny rules do not block Bash subprocesses:** A `Read(./.env)` deny rule blocks the Read tool but does not prevent `cat .env` in Bash. OS-level enforcement requires enabling the sandbox.

**Symlink allow rules require both paths to match:** A symlink inside an allowed directory pointing outside it still prompts. Deny rules block when either path matches.

**`dontAsk` mode and explicit `ask` rules:** Explicit ask rules are denied rather than prompting. There is no interactive fallback.

**Linux sandbox strength:** The Linux implementation includes `enableWeakerNestedSandbox` mode for Docker environments without privileged namespaces. This "considerably weakens security."

**Domain fronting:** Network filtering restricts domains but does not inspect traffic. Domain fronting can bypass network filtering.

**`allowUnixSockets` risk:** Allowing `/var/run/docker.sock` via `allowUnixSockets` effectively grants host system access through the Docker socket.

**Broad filesystem write permissions risk:** Allowing writes to directories containing executables in `$PATH`, system config directories, or shell config files enables privilege escalation.


---

## §10. Subagent hook events

### Scope

Every hook event that fires during or because of a subagent's lifecycle. The canonical subagent-specific events are `SubagentStart` and `SubagentStop`. Two additional events touch the subagent boundary directly: `WorktreeRemove` (fires when a worktree-isolated subagent finishes) and `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` on the `Agent` tool (fire in the orchestrator when Claude spawns a subagent). All other hook events — when fired inside a subagent's own execution — receive augmented common fields (`agent_id`, `agent_type`) identifying the subagent context.

### Specification

#### Common input fields — subagent augmentation

When any hook fires inside a subagent execution, two extra fields appear alongside the standard common fields:

> "When running with `--agent` or inside a subagent, two additional fields are included" [hooks §Common input fields](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#common-input-fields)

| Field        | Description |
|:-------------|:------------|
| `agent_id`   | "Unique identifier for the subagent. Present only when the hook fires inside a subagent call. Use this to distinguish subagent hook calls from main-thread calls." |
| `agent_type` | "Agent name (for example, `'Explore'` or `'security-reviewer'`). Present when the session uses `--agent` or the hook fires inside a subagent. For subagents, the subagent's type takes precedence over the session's `--agent` value." |

#### SubagentStart

**When it fires:** "When a Claude Code subagent is spawned via the Agent tool."

**Matcher:** Filters on agent type name. Valid values: `Bash`, `Explore`, `Plan`, or custom agent names from `.claude/agents/`.

**Supports prompt/agent hook types:** No. SubagentStart supports `command` and `http` hooks only (not `prompt` or `agent`).

##### JSON input

| Field        | Description |
|:-------------|:------------|
| `agent_id`   | "Unique identifier for the subagent" |
| `agent_type` | "The agent name (built-in agents like `'Bash'`, `'Explore'`, `'Plan'`, or custom agent names)" |

```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStart",
  "agent_id": "agent-abc123",
  "agent_type": "Explore"
}
```

##### JSON output / decision control

SubagentStart **cannot block** subagent creation. Exit code 2 shows stderr to the user only; it does not prevent the subagent from spawning.

| Field               | Description |
|:--------------------|:------------|
| `additionalContext` | "String added to the subagent's context" |

```json
{
  "hookSpecificOutput": {
    "hookEventName": "SubagentStart",
    "additionalContext": "Follow security guidelines for this task"
  }
}
```

##### Exit code 2 behavior

> "SubagentStart — Can block? No — Shows stderr to user only" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

The subagent spawns regardless. Stderr is surfaced to the user, not Claude.

#### SubagentStop

**When it fires:** "When a Claude Code subagent has finished responding."

**Matcher:** Filters on agent type. "Same values as SubagentStart."

**Supports prompt/agent hook types:** Yes. SubagentStop is in the set of events that support all four hook types: `command`, `http`, `prompt`, and `agent`.

##### JSON input

| Field                    | Description |
|:-------------------------|:------------|
| `stop_hook_active`       | `true` when the subagent is already continuing as a result of a SubagentStop hook. Check this to prevent infinite loops. |
| `agent_id`               | The subagent's unique identifier. |
| `agent_type`             | "The value used for matcher filtering." |
| `agent_transcript_path`  | "The subagent's own transcript stored in a nested `subagents/` folder." |
| `last_assistant_message` | "The text content of the subagent's final response, so hooks can access it without parsing the transcript file." |

```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123.jsonl",
  "cwd": "/Users/...",
  "permission_mode": "default",
  "hook_event_name": "SubagentStop",
  "stop_hook_active": false,
  "agent_id": "def456",
  "agent_type": "Explore",
  "agent_transcript_path": "~/.claude/projects/.../abc123/subagents/agent-def456.jsonl",
  "last_assistant_message": "Analysis complete. Found 3 potential issues..."
}
```

##### JSON output / decision control

> "SubagentStop hooks use the same decision control format as Stop hooks." [hooks §SubagentStop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#subagentstop)

| Field      | Description |
|:-----------|:------------|
| `decision` | `"block"` prevents the subagent from stopping. Omit to allow it to stop. |
| `reason`   | "Required when `decision` is `'block'`. Tells Claude why it should continue." |

##### Exit code 2 behavior

> "SubagentStop — Can block? Yes — Prevents the subagent from stopping" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

The subagent receives the stderr message as feedback and continues working.

##### Stop hook conversion for subagents in frontmatter

> "For subagents, `Stop` hooks are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` since that is the event that fires when a subagent completes." [hooks §Hooks in skills and agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#hooks-in-skills-and-agents)

This conversion applies only when hooks are defined in subagent frontmatter, not in settings files.

#### WorktreeRemove (subagent-relevant trigger)

**When it fires:** "When a worktree is being removed, either at session exit or when a subagent finishes."

The subagent-specific trigger: when "a subagent with `isolation: 'worktree'` finishes."

**Supports prompt/agent hook types:** No. WorktreeRemove supports `command` and `http` hooks only.

##### JSON input

| Field           | Description |
|:----------------|:------------|
| `worktree_path` | "The absolute path to the worktree being removed." |

##### Decision control

"WorktreeRemove hooks have no decision control. They cannot block worktree removal but can perform cleanup tasks like removing version control state or archiving changes."

##### Exit code 2 behavior

> "WorktreeRemove — Can block? No — Failures are logged in debug mode only" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

Non-blocking. Errors do not surface to the user or Claude.

#### PreToolUse on the Agent tool (orchestrator side)

When the orchestrator calls the `Agent` tool to spawn a subagent, `PreToolUse` fires in the orchestrator context — before the subagent exists.

**Matcher:** `Agent`

**Tool input fields specific to Agent:**

| Field           | Type   | Example                    | Description |
|:----------------|:-------|:---------------------------|:------------|
| `prompt`        | string | `"Find all API endpoints"` | "The task for the agent to perform" |
| `description`   | string | `"Find API endpoints"`     | "Short description of the task" |
| `subagent_type` | string | `"Explore"`                | "Type of specialized agent to use" |
| `model`         | string | `"sonnet"`                 | "Optional model alias to override the default" |

A `PreToolUse` hook on `Agent` can deny the tool call with `permissionDecision: "deny"`, preventing the subagent from being created at all.

#### PostToolUse on the Agent tool (orchestrator side)

Fires in the orchestrator after the subagent completes and returns its result. The tool has already executed; the subagent is done.

**Decision control:** `PostToolUse` on `Agent` uses the standard top-level `decision: "block"` pattern. This feeds feedback to the orchestrator Claude, not the subagent (which has already finished).

### Behavior

#### Lifecycle ordering

The hooks reference lifecycle diagram shows the sequence inside the per-turn agentic loop:

> "the nested agentic loop (PreToolUse, PermissionRequest, PostToolUse, SubagentStart/Stop, TaskCreated, TaskCompleted)" [hooks §Hook lifecycle](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#hook-lifecycle)

Order when Claude spawns a subagent:

1. `PreToolUse` (matcher: `Agent`) — fires in orchestrator before spawn
2. `SubagentStart` — fires when subagent is spawned
3. [subagent executes; its own tool calls fire `PreToolUse`/`PostToolUse` inside the subagent, tagged with `agent_id` and `agent_type`]
4. `SubagentStop` — fires when subagent finishes
5. `PostToolUse` (matcher: `Agent`) — fires in orchestrator after subagent completes
6. `WorktreeRemove` — fires if the subagent used `isolation: "worktree"`

#### Hook types supported per event

| Event           | command | http | prompt | agent |
|:----------------|:-------:|:----:|:------:|:-----:|
| SubagentStart   | yes     | yes  | no     | no    |
| SubagentStop    | yes     | yes  | yes    | yes   |
| WorktreeRemove  | yes     | yes  | no     | no    |

#### Infinite loop guard

SubagentStop includes `stop_hook_active` for the same reason Stop does. The docs describe the pattern for Stop:

> "Check this value or process the transcript to prevent Claude Code from running indefinitely." [hooks §Stop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#stop)

The same guard applies to SubagentStop. Parse `stop_hook_active` and exit 0 immediately if it is `true`.

#### Hooks defined in subagent frontmatter

> "All hook events are supported. For subagents, `Stop` hooks are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` since that is the event that fires when a subagent completes." [hooks §Hooks in skills and agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#hooks-in-skills-and-agents)

> "Hooks use the same configuration format as settings-based hooks but are scoped to the component's lifetime and cleaned up when it finishes." [hooks §Hooks in skills and agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#hooks-in-skills-and-agents)

The `once` field on hook handlers applies to skills but not agents:

> "`once` — If `true`, runs only once per session then is removed. Skills only, not agents." [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

#### Where hooks fire relative to the subagent

Any hook event that fires inside a subagent's tool calls (e.g., `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `Notification`) receives `agent_id` and `agent_type` in its common input. This allows a single hook script registered in settings to distinguish whether it is running in the main thread or inside a subagent.

### Edge cases

#### Blocking SubagentStart does not prevent spawn

Exit code 2 on `SubagentStart` shows stderr to the user only. The subagent spawns regardless. There is no supported mechanism to block subagent creation from a `SubagentStart` hook. To block creation, use `PreToolUse` with matcher `Agent` before the subagent exists.

#### WorktreeRemove errors are invisible

Failures from `WorktreeRemove` hooks are "logged in debug mode only." They do not surface to the user or Claude. A broken cleanup hook silently leaves worktree state on disk.

#### WorktreeCreate hook replaces default git behavior entirely

> "Because the hook replaces the default behavior entirely, `.worktreeinclude` is not processed." [hooks §WorktreeCreate](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#worktreecreate)

If a `WorktreeCreate` hook is configured and a subagent uses `isolation: "worktree"`, the hook must handle all setup including copying local configuration files. Omitting that step silently misconfigures the subagent environment.

#### `agent_type` precedence when `--agent` is set

> "For subagents, the subagent's type takes precedence over the session's `--agent` value." [hooks §Common input fields](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#common-input-fields)

A session launched with `claude --agent security-reviewer` that spawns an `Explore` subagent will have `agent_type: "Explore"` in all hooks firing inside that subagent, not `"security-reviewer"`.

#### `once` field does not apply to agent frontmatter hooks

The `once: true` field only applies to skill-defined hooks. Agent-defined hooks do not support it and will fire on every matching occurrence during the agent's lifetime.

#### Stop hooks in subagent frontmatter are silently rewritten

A `Stop` hook written in an agent's frontmatter never fires as `Stop`. It fires as `SubagentStop`. The conversion is automatic and silent; the `hook_event_name` field in the JSON input will say `SubagentStop`.

#### Async hooks cannot control subagent behavior

> "Async hooks cannot block tool calls or return decisions. By the time the hook completes, the triggering action has already proceeded." [hooks §Limitations](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#limitations)

An `async: true` hook on `SubagentStop` cannot block the subagent from stopping.


---

## §11. Plugin packaging for agents

### Scope

How agent definitions ship inside a Claude Code plugin: the directory convention, the `plugin.json` fields that govern agent packaging, how auto-discovery works, how marketplace entries interact with plugin-level agent declarations, the available plugin source types and their fields, and how version constraints apply to agent-bearing plugins.

### Specification

#### Plugin directory structure for agents

A plugin is a self-contained directory. The manifest lives at `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`. All component directories — including `agents/` — sit at the **plugin root**, not inside `.claude-plugin/`.

```
my-plugin/
├── .claude-plugin/
│   └── plugin.json          ← manifest only
├── agents/                  ← agent definitions (default location)
│   ├── security-reviewer.md
│   └── compliance-checker.md
├── skills/
├── hooks/
└── ...
```

> "All other directories (commands/, agents/, skills/, hooks/) must be at the plugin root, not inside `.claude-plugin/`." [plugins-reference §Standard plugin layout](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#standard-plugin-layout)

#### Auto-discovery

Claude Code discovers agents automatically from the default `agents/` directory at the plugin root. No explicit registration in `plugin.json` is required.

> "Skills and commands are automatically discovered when the plugin is installed." [plugins-reference §Skills](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#skills)

The same auto-discovery applies to agents; the reference lists `agents/` in the standard layout without requiring manifest registration.

#### Agent file format inside a plugin

Each agent is a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter. Supported frontmatter fields for plugin-shipped agents:

| Frontmatter field   | Notes |
|---------------------|-------|
| `name`              | Agent identifier; combined with plugin `name` for namespaced display (e.g., `<plugin-name>:<agent-name>`) |
| `description`       | When to invoke the agent; Claude uses this for automatic dispatch |
| `model`             | e.g., `sonnet` |
| `effort`            | e.g., `medium` |
| `maxTurns`          | Integer |
| `tools`             | Allowlist |
| `disallowedTools`   | e.g., `Write, Edit` |
| `skills`            | Skills the agent may invoke |
| `memory`            |       |
| `background`        |       |
| `isolation`         | Only valid value: `"worktree"` |

Security-restricted fields — **not supported** for plugin-shipped agents: `hooks`, `mcpServers`, `permissionMode`.

> "Plugin agents support `name`, `description`, `model`, `effort`, `maxTurns`, `tools`, `disallowedTools`, `skills`, `memory`, `background`, and `isolation` frontmatter fields. The only valid `isolation` value is `\"worktree\"`. For security reasons, `hooks`, `mcpServers`, and `permissionMode` are not supported for plugin-shipped agents." [plugins-reference §Agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#agents)

#### `plugin.json` fields relevant to agent packaging

The complete `plugin.json` schema [plugins-reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference) includes these agent-relevant fields:

**Required field (when manifest is present):**

| Field  | Type   | Description |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| `name` | string | Unique identifier, kebab-case. Becomes the namespace prefix for all plugin components. |

**Component path override:**

| Field    | Type          | Description |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| `agents` | string\|array | Custom path(s) to agent files. **Replaces** the default `agents/` directory — the default is not scanned when this field is set. |

Path rules for `agents`:

- Must be relative to the plugin root and start with `./`
- Accepts a single path string or an array of paths
- To keep the default directory and add more, include it explicitly: `"agents": ["./agents/", "./extras/"]`
- Multiple specific files can be listed: `"agents": ["./custom-agents/reviewer.md", "./custom-agents/tester.md"]`

> "For `skills`, `commands`, `agents`, `outputStyles`, and `monitors`, a custom path replaces the default." [plugins-reference §Path behavior rules](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#path-behavior-rules)

**Settings integration:**

The plugin root may also contain `settings.json`. Only `agent` and `subagentStatusLine` keys are currently supported there. The `agent` key activates a specific plugin agent as the main thread:

```json
{
  "agent": "security-reviewer"
}
```

This sets the named agent (from the plugin's `agents/` directory) as the default agent for sessions where the plugin is active. `settings.json` at the plugin root takes priority over a `settings` key in `plugin.json`.

> "Setting `agent` activates one of the plugin's custom agents as the main thread, applying its system prompt, tool restrictions, and model. … Settings from `settings.json` take priority over `settings` declared in `plugin.json`. Unknown keys are silently ignored." [plugins §Ship default settings with you](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins#ship-default-settings-with-your-plugin)

**The manifest is optional:**

> "The manifest is optional. If omitted, Claude Code auto-discovers components in default locations and derives the plugin name from the directory name. Use a manifest when you need to provide metadata or custom component paths." [plugins-reference §Plugin manifest schema](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#plugin-manifest-schema)

#### Namespacing

The plugin `name` field is the namespace for all components. An agent named `agent-creator` in a plugin named `<plugin-name>` appears in the UI and is referenced as `<plugin-name>:agent-creator`.

> "This name is used for namespacing components." [plugins-reference §Required fields](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#required-fields)

#### Marketplace entry fields for agent-bearing plugins

The `marketplace.json` plugin entry supports an `agents` field parallel to the `plugin.json` field:

| Field    | Type          | Description |
|----------|---------------|-------------|
| `agents` | string\|array | Custom paths to agent files in the plugin source. Same semantics as the `plugin.json` field. |

The `strict` field governs which source is authoritative:

| Value            | Behavior |
|------------------|----------|
| `true` (default) | `plugin.json` is authoritative. The marketplace entry can supplement with additional components; both are merged. |
| `false`          | The marketplace entry is the entire definition. If the plugin also has a `plugin.json` that declares components, the conflict causes the plugin to fail to load. |

> "You can include any field from the plugin manifest schema (like `description`, `version`, `author`, `commands`, `hooks`, etc.), plus these marketplace-specific fields: `source`, `category`, `tags`, and `strict`." [plugin-marketplaces](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces)

> "`strict: false`: Since this is set to false, the plugin doesn't need its own `plugin.json`. The marketplace entry defines everything." [plugin-marketplaces](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces)

#### Plugin source types

Sources are set in the `source` field of each plugin entry in `marketplace.json`.

| Source type    | `source` value  | Required fields          | Optional fields                        |
|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Relative path  | string (`"./…"`) | —                       | —                                      |
| GitHub         | `"github"`      | `repo` (`owner/repo`)    | `ref` (branch/tag), `sha` (40-char)   |
| Git URL        | `"url"`         | `url`                    | `ref`, `sha`                           |
| Git subdirectory | `"git-subdir"` | `url`, `path`           | `ref`, `sha`                           |
| npm            | `"npm"`         | `package`                | `version` (semver range), `registry`   |

Notes on specific types:

- Relative paths resolve relative to the marketplace root (the directory containing `.claude-plugin/`), not relative to `marketplace.json` itself. They work only when the marketplace is added via git; URL-based marketplace additions cannot resolve relative paths.
- `git-subdir` uses sparse checkout to minimize bandwidth for monorepos.
- `npm` `version` accepts semver ranges (e.g., `^2.0.0`, `~1.5.0`). The `registry` field defaults to the system npm registry.

#### Version constraints for agent-bearing plugins

Version constraints apply through the `dependencies` array in `plugin.json`:

```json
{
  "name": "deploy-kit",
  "version": "3.1.0",
  "dependencies": [
    "audit-logger",
    { "name": "secrets-vault", "version": "~2.1.0" }
  ]
}
```

Each entry is either a bare plugin name string (tracks latest) or an object with:

| Field       | Type   | Description |
|-------------|--------|-------------|
| `name`      | string | Plugin name. Resolves within the same marketplace as the declaring plugin. Required. |
| `version`   | string | Any Node `semver` range expression: `~2.1.0`, `^2.0`, `>=1.4`, `=2.1.0`. Pre-release versions excluded unless range opts in with a pre-release suffix. |
| `marketplace` | string | Different marketplace to resolve `name` in. Blocked unless the target is allowlisted in the root marketplace's `marketplace.json`. |

Version resolution uses git tags in the format `{plugin-name}--v{version}` on the marketplace repository. Claude Code fetches the highest tagged version satisfying the declared range.

For `npm` sources: tag-based resolution does not apply. The constraint is checked at load time; if the installed version does not satisfy it, the dependent plugin is disabled with `dependency-version-unsatisfied`.

> "List dependencies in the `dependencies` array of your plugin's `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`." [plugin-dependencies §Declare a dependency with a ve](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-dependencies#declare-a-dependency-with-a-version-constraint)

Requires Claude Code v2.1.110 or later.

### Behavior

- When a plugin is installed from a marketplace, Claude Code copies it to the plugin cache at `~/.claude/plugins/cache`. Agents in the cached copy are loaded from there, not from the source location.
- Agents appear in the `/agents` interface and can be invoked manually or automatically by Claude based on their `description`.
- Plugin agents work alongside built-in Claude agents.
- Running `/reload-plugins` inside a session picks up changes to agent files without restarting.
- When `--plugin-dir` loads a plugin locally (development mode), it takes precedence over an installed marketplace plugin of the same name, except for marketplace plugins force-enabled by managed settings.
- Each installed version occupies a separate cache directory. Orphaned versions (after update or uninstall) are removed automatically after 7 days. Glob and Grep tools skip orphaned directories.
- When a plugin is uninstalled from the last scope where it was installed, the `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA}` directory is deleted unless `--keep-data` is passed.
- Disabling a plugin does not stop already-running monitors; that applies to monitors, not agents directly, but is relevant when an agent activates monitors.

### Edge cases

**Custom path replaces default:** Setting `"agents": "./custom-agents/"` in `plugin.json` disables scanning of the default `agents/` directory entirely. To keep the default and add more paths, list both explicitly.

**Manifest is optional but `name` is required when manifest exists:** A plugin without any `plugin.json` derives its name from the directory name. Once you create `plugin.json`, `name` becomes the only required field.

**Version set in both `plugin.json` and marketplace entry:** `plugin.json` wins silently. The marketplace `version` field is ignored. For relative-path plugins, set version only in the marketplace entry. For all other sources, set it in the manifest.

**`strict: false` conflict:** If `strict: false` is set in the marketplace entry and the plugin also has a `plugin.json` that declares components (skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers, output styles), the plugin fails to load with a conflict error.

**Same-name plugin via `--plugin-dir`:** Local development copy takes precedence over installed marketplace copy for that session. Exception: marketplace plugins force-enabled by managed settings cannot be overridden.

**`isolation: "worktree"` is the only valid isolation value** for plugin agents. Any other value is rejected.

**Cross-marketplace dependencies:** Blocked by default unless the target marketplace is explicitly allowlisted in the root marketplace's `marketplace.json`.

**`no-matching-tag` error:** If the upstream plugin has no git tags in the `{name}--v{version}` format satisfying the declared range, the dependent plugin is disabled. Tag-based resolution applies only to git-backed sources; npm sources check the constraint at load time instead.


---

## Appendix A — Consolidated footguns

Severity tiers:

- **Critical**: silently breaks something or causes unrecoverable damage.
- **Severe**: surprising semantic that violates intuition; high blast radius.
- **Moderate**: under-documented behavior; bites authors who do not know it.
- **Minor**: style, nomenclature, or low-impact quirk.

Within each tier, footguns are grouped by the primary section they belong to.

### Critical

#### CR-1. Plugin agents silently drop `hooks`, `mcpServers`, and `permissionMode` (§1, §2, §4, §6, §7, §8, §11)

A subagent file that works correctly in `.claude/agents/` silently loses three of its frontmatter fields when distributed via a plugin. The plugin loader does not error or warn — it ignores the fields. A subagent depending on a `PreToolUse` hook to enforce read-only behavior, on inline `mcpServers` for restricted DB access, or on `permissionMode: dontAsk` for safety becomes a permissive agent when shipped through a plugin. A `Stop` hook in plugin agent frontmatter (which would normally rewrite to `SubagentStop`) is also dropped, removing the return-path observer entirely.

> "For security reasons, plugin subagents do not support the `hooks`, `mcpServers`, or `permissionMode` frontmatter fields. These fields are ignored when loading agents from a plugin." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

> "For security reasons, `hooks`, `mcpServers`, and `permissionMode` are not supported for plugin-shipped agents." [plugins-reference §Agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#agents)

#### CR-2. SubagentStop block-and-continue can loop indefinitely if `stop_hook_active` is ignored (§8, §10)

A `SubagentStop` hook that returns `decision: "block"` whenever a quality gate fails will keep firing on every subsequent `SubagentStop` triggered by the continuation. The `stop_hook_active` field is the only documented escape; primary sources do not state a built-in retry cap for `SubagentStop`. The docs address this footgun for `Stop` hooks in the troubleshooting guide but do not repeat the warning for `SubagentStop` in its own section. A naive quality-gate hook becomes an infinite loop the orchestrator cannot observe (the loop happens inside the subagent).

> "SubagentStop hooks receive `stop_hook_active`, `agent_id`, `agent_type`, `agent_transcript_path`, and `last_assistant_message`." [hooks §SubagentStop](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#subagentstop)

> "Your Stop hook script needs to check whether it already triggered a continuation. Parse the `stop_hook_active` field from the JSON input and exit early if it's `true`" [hooks-guide §Stop hook runs forever](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide#stop-hook-runs-forever)

### Severe

#### SV-1. Parent `bypassPermissions` or `acceptEdits` silently overrides subagent `permissionMode` (§1, §4, §6, §7, §8, §9)

A subagent author who sets `permissionMode: default` expecting safety prompts will silently lose them when the parent runs in `bypassPermissions`. The subagent's restrictive declaration is ignored; the parent's permissive mode propagates with no warning. This inverts the usual "subagent declares its own mode" assumption.

> "If the parent uses `bypassPermissions` or `acceptEdits`, this takes precedence and cannot be overridden." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### SV-2. Parent `auto` mode silently overrides subagent `permissionMode` entirely (§1, §4, §7, §9)

Distinct from SV-1, `auto` mode propagation is a third independent override path. The subagent's `permissionMode` field is completely ignored when the parent runs in `auto`, and the classifier uses the parent's `autoMode.allow`/`autoMode.soft_deny` lists — not anything declared in the subagent.

> "If the parent uses auto mode, the subagent inherits auto mode and any `permissionMode` in its frontmatter is ignored: the classifier evaluates the subagent's tool calls with the same block and allow rules as the parent session." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### SV-3. `Agent(agent_type)` in subagent definitions has no effect — subagents cannot spawn subagents (§1, §4, §6, §7)

The `tools` field in a subagent definition accepts `Agent(worker)` syntax without erroring even when listed in a subagent definition, but the entry is silently ineffective. Subagents cannot spawn other subagents regardless of tool configuration. The author believes a security boundary or orchestration capability exists where none does. Orchestration-style nesting must happen from the main conversation.

> "This restriction only applies to agents running as the main thread with `claude --agent`. Subagents cannot spawn other subagents, so `Agent(agent_type)` has no effect in subagent definitions."
