[sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### SV-4. Subagents inherit ALL tools by default — including MCP and Bash (§4)

Authors who write a tightly-scoped subagent and forget the `tools:` field get a maximally-privileged agent. The frontmatter table's "Required: No" for `tools` masks this — "optional" is read as "safe to omit." MCP tool inclusion in the inheritance is the second surprise: a subagent designed for code review may silently inherit access to a database-write MCP server configured in the parent session.

> "By default, subagents inherit all tools from the main conversation, including MCP tools." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### SV-5. `disallowedTools` applies BEFORE `tools` — listed-in-both is removed (§1, §4, §6)

The intuitive expectation is that an allowlist (`tools`) is sufficient. The actual semantics are sequential subtraction: a tool listed in `tools: Read, Edit` and `disallowedTools: Edit` ends up with only `Read` available. The "tool listed in both is removed" rule means you cannot "force-allow" a tool by listing it in both fields.

> "If both are set, `disallowedTools` is applied first, then `tools` is resolved against the remaining pool. A tool listed in both is removed." [sub-agents §Define hooks for subagents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#define-hooks-for-subagents)

#### SV-6. `memory` unconditionally re-enables Read, Write, and Edit — even if `disallowedTools` excluded them (§1)

An author who writes `disallowedTools: Write, Edit` to prevent the agent from modifying files, and then adds `memory: project` to gain knowledge persistence, may not realize memory unconditionally re-enables Write and Edit. Primary sources state the enablement is automatic but do not specify how this interacts with explicit `disallowedTools` exclusions.

> "Read, Write, and Edit tools are automatically enabled so the subagent can manage its memory files." [sub-agents §Invoke subagents explicitly](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#invoke-subagents-explicitly)

#### SV-7. Frontmatter hooks fire only as a subagent — silent in `--agent` main-session mode (§1, §6)

The same definition file carries the hooks. In main-session mode, its frontmatter hooks are dead. An author testing the hooks while the agent is a subagent will see them fire; switching to `--agent` mode silently disables them.

> "Frontmatter hooks fire when the agent is spawned as a subagent through the Agent tool or an @-mention. They do not fire when the agent runs as the main session via `--agent` or the `agent` setting." [sub-agents §Write subagent files](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#write-subagent-files)

#### SV-8. `--agent` replaces the entire Claude Code system prompt (§3, §6)

`--agent` is not a routing preference; it removes the default Claude Code system prompt and installs the subagent's prompt as the main session prompt. Using `--agent` with a narrowly-scoped subagent (e.g., one with read-only tools) silently restricts all session capabilities, not just the tasks the subagent was designed for.

> "The subagent's system prompt replaces the default Claude Code system prompt entirely, the same way `--system-prompt` does." [sub-agents §Write subagent files](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#write-subagent-files)

#### SV-9. Subagent does not receive the Claude Code system prompt (§6, §7)

Authors who expect a subagent to behave like the main Claude Code session — following its global instructions, tool guidance, and safety heuristics — will find the subagent stripped of all that context. Only the definition body, the working directory, optional skill injections, and the memory prefix arrive. Any behavior the parent Claude Code system prompt enforced must be re-stated explicitly in the subagent's markdown body.

> "Subagents receive only this system prompt (plus basic environment details like working directory), not the full Claude Code system prompt." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

#### SV-10. Parent conversation history and auto memory are invisible to the subagent (§7)

Users often phrase requests with implicit reference to the prior turn ("now do that for the auth module"). When Claude delegates such a request to a subagent, the subagent receives only what Claude writes into the task prompt — not the user's verbatim message, not the prior turn, not the file Claude read three turns ago. Project auto memory (`MEMORY.md` in `~/.claude/projects/...`) is also not inherited; a subagent with `memory:` frontmatter loads its OWN separate `MEMORY.md`, which may be empty or contain unrelated learnings.

> "starts without your conversation history or the main session's auto memory." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window)

> "The main session's auto memory is not included. If a custom agent has memory: in its frontmatter, it loads its own separate MEMORY.md here instead." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window)

#### SV-11. Built-in Explore and Plan skip CLAUDE.md entirely (§5, §7)

Authors writing CLAUDE.md to encode project conventions may assume Claude's built-in research agents respect those conventions. Explore and Plan do not. Naming conventions, architectural rules, and "always do X" directives in CLAUDE.md will not influence Explore's or Plan's behavior. Custom subagents and the general-purpose subagent do load CLAUDE.md.

> "The subagent loads CLAUDE.md too. Same file, same content, but it counts against the subagent's context, not yours. The built-in Explore and Plan agents skip this for a smaller context." [context-window](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window)

#### SV-12. Working directory inherited but `cd` is sandboxed per Bash call (§7)

A subagent doing multi-step shell work may write `cd src && npm install` in one Bash call and `npm test` in the next, expecting the directory change to persist. It does not. Each Bash call starts back at the inherited working directory. The fix is to chain commands with `&&` in a single tool call or to use absolute paths.

> "Within a subagent, `cd` commands do not persist between Bash or PowerShell tool calls and do not affect the main conversation's working directory." [sub-agents §Common patterns](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#common-patterns)

#### SV-13. The orchestrator never sees the subagent's transcript — only a summary (§8)

An author who designs a subagent on the assumption that the orchestrator can post-hoc inspect the subagent's tool calls or intermediate reasoning will find that those records sit in a separate JSONL file under `~/.claude/projects/{project}/{sessionId}/subagents/agent-{agentId}.jsonl`. The orchestrator's transcript receives the summary text and the agent ID. Anything the subagent failed to surface in its final assistant message is invisible to downstream prompts unless the parent reads the subagent transcript file from disk explicitly.

> "the subagent does that work in its own context and returns only the summary." [sub-agents §Define hooks for subagents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#define-hooks-for-subagents)

#### SV-14. No documented `SubagentStopFailure` event (§8)

`StopFailure` is documented for the main agent only. The hook-events table lists no `SubagentStopFailure`. An author wiring a "catch any subagent crash" hook has no documented event for API errors / rate limits / `max_output_tokens` originating inside a subagent. Whether `SubagentStop` fires at all in those cases is not stated.

> "`StopFailure` | When the turn ends due to an API error. Output and exit code are ignored" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

#### SV-15. No documented token budget on the returned summary (§8)

The "isolation" framing (subagent context is separate, doesn't bloat parent) is true *until* the subagent returns. There is no documented cap on how large the returned summary can be. The 10,000-character cap on hook-injected context does not govern the subagent return value. Fan-out designs with N parallel subagents can pay N × (uncapped summary) into the orchestrator on completion.

> "When subagents complete, their results return to your main conversation. Running many subagents that each return detailed results can consume significant context." [sub-agents §Run subagents in foreground or](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#run-subagents-in-foreground-or-background)

#### SV-16. Frontmatter `Stop` silently rewrites to `SubagentStop` (§8, §10)

The author writes `Stop:` in the agent's frontmatter expecting the hook to fire when the *session* stops. The runtime rewrites it to `SubagentStop`, which fires when *this subagent* finishes. The `hook_event_name` field the hook receives says `SubagentStop`, not `Stop`. The conversion is automatic, silent, and undocumented in the event section itself.

> "`Stop` hooks in frontmatter are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` events." [sub-agents §Choose a model](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-a-model)

#### SV-17. SubagentStart exit 2 does NOT block subagent spawn (§10)

`SubagentStart` sounds like a pre-spawn gate. Authors expect to validate the agent type and abort if it fails validation. The hook fires, the script exits 2 with a clear error message — and the subagent spawns anyway. Stderr goes to the user only, not to Claude. To block creation, use `PreToolUse` with matcher `Agent` before the subagent exists.

> "SubagentStart — Can block? No — Shows stderr to user only" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

#### SV-18. Async SubagentStop hooks cannot prevent subagent stop (§10)

`SubagentStop` is listed as a blocking event (exit code 2 prevents the subagent from stopping). Adding `"async": true` to a `SubagentStop` hook loses all blocking capability without any validation error or warning. A hook meant to enforce a quality gate before the subagent stops becomes a fire-and-forget logging hook. The `decision: "block"` output field is silently ignored.

> "Async hooks cannot block tool calls or return decisions. By the time the hook completes, the triggering action has already proceeded." [hooks §Limitations](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#limitations)

#### SV-19. WorktreeRemove failures are completely invisible (§10)

WorktreeRemove is a cleanup hook. If it fails — perhaps because it cannot remove a lock file or an SVN checkout — the failure is logged only when debug mode is active. No user-visible error. No Claude-visible feedback. The subagent has already finished; the worktree may be left in a broken state.

> "WorktreeRemove — Can block? No — Failures are logged in debug mode only" [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

#### SV-20. WorktreeCreate hook omits `.worktreeinclude` processing (§10)

Authors who use `.worktreeinclude` to manage local-only files (environment secrets, tool configs) and also configure a `WorktreeCreate` hook for a non-git VCS will find those files absent in the subagent's worktree. The hook replaces, not extends, the default behavior. The subagent operates in a misconfigured environment with no error.

> "Because the hook replaces the default behavior entirely, `.worktreeinclude` is not processed. If you need to copy local configuration files like `.env` into the new worktree, do it inside your hook script." [hooks §WorktreeCreate](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#worktreecreate)

#### SV-21. MCP tool naming uses double underscore — not single colon, single underscore, or double colon (§4)

Three other separator styles appear in nearby Claude Code documentation, inviting confusion: (a) MCP **resources** use a single colon (`@server:protocol://resource/path`); (b) Bash trailing wildcard supports `:*`; (c) Plugin subagent typeahead uses single colon (`<plugin-name>:<agent-name>`). The MCP **tool/prompt** namespace alone uses `mcp__<server>__<tool>` with a literal lowercase `mcp` prefix and double-underscore separators between every segment.

> "`mcp__puppeteer__puppeteer_navigate` matches the `puppeteer_navigate` tool provided by the `puppeteer` server" [permissions §MCP](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#mcp)

MCP server names are also auto-normalized — spaces become underscores. A server registered as `"My Server"` becomes `My_Server` in tool names; a permission rule like `mcp__My Server` will not match.

> "Server and prompt names are normalized (spaces become underscores)" [mcp §Execute MCP prompts](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp#execute-mcp-prompts)

#### SV-22. Read/Edit deny rules do NOT block equivalent Bash commands (§4, §9)

A safety-conscious config that adds `"deny": ["Read(./.env)"]` is useless against `Bash(cat .env)` if Bash is allowed. The deny rule semantics are tool-bound, not file-bound. OS-level path enforcement requires the sandbox.

> "Read and Edit deny rules apply to Claude's built-in file tools, not to Bash subprocesses. A `Read(./.env)` deny rule blocks the Read tool but does not prevent `cat .env` in Bash." [permissions §Read and Edit](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#read-and-edit)

#### SV-23. Bash constraint patterns are fragile for argument restriction (§4, §9)

Argument reordering, environment variables, protocol changes, or extra spaces bypass `Bash(curl http://github.com/ *)`. Three of the bypasses (options-before-URL, different protocol, extra spaces) are not adversarial; they are normal command-line variation. URL-restriction logic must move to `WebFetch(domain:...)` or PreToolUse hooks.

> "`Bash(curl http://github.com/ *)` intends to restrict curl to GitHub URLs, but won't match variations like: Options before URL: `curl -X GET http://github.com/...`; Different protocol: `curl https://github.com/...`; Variables: `URL=http://github.com && curl $URL`" [permissions §Bash](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#bash)

#### SV-24. Path patterns starting with single `/` are project-relative, NOT absolute (§4, §9)

`Read(/etc/passwd)` reads `<project-root>/etc/passwd`, not `/etc/passwd`. The double-leading-slash `//` is the absolute form, mimicking gitignore semantics rather than POSIX paths. Sandbox `filesystem.*` settings use the opposite convention (POSIX): `/tmp/build` is absolute there, while in permission rules `/tmp/build` is project-relative.

> "A pattern like `/Users/alice/file` is NOT an absolute path. It's relative to the project root. Use `//Users/alice/file` for absolute paths." [permissions §Read and Edit](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#read-and-edit)

> "This syntax differs from Read and Edit permission rules, which use `//path` for absolute and `/path` for project-relative. Sandbox filesystem paths use standard conventions: `/tmp/build` is an absolute path." [sandboxing](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing)

#### SV-25. Built-in read-only Bash commands run without prompt in EVERY mode (§4)

A subagent with `tools: Bash` in `default` permission mode still allows `cat`/`grep`/`find`/`ls`/`head`/`tail`/`wc`/`diff`/`stat`/`du`/`cd` and read-only `git` without any prompt. The set is not configurable; only an `ask` or `deny` rule can re-enable prompting.

> "Claude Code recognizes a built-in set of Bash commands as read-only and runs them without a permission prompt in every mode... The set is not configurable; to require a prompt for one of these commands, add an `ask` or `deny` rule for it." [permissions §Bash](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#bash)

#### SV-26. Sandbox auto-allow overrides `ask: Bash(*)` (§4, §9)

An author adds `ask: Bash(*)` to force confirmation on every Bash invocation, then enables sandboxing for defense-in-depth, and the prompts disappear. The two safety layers interact: the sandbox is treated as sufficient confinement, so the per-call prompt is suppressed.

> "When sandboxing is enabled with `autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`, which is the default, sandboxed Bash commands run without prompting even if your permissions include `ask: Bash(*)`." [permissions §How permissions interact with](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#how-permissions-interact-with-sandboxing)

#### SV-27. `bypassPermissions` carve-outs for `.claude/agents` writes are silent (§1, §4, §9)

Despite `.claude` being a protected directory, writes to `.claude/commands`, `.claude/agents`, and `.claude/skills` are explicitly exempt from prompting in all modes including `bypassPermissions`. A prompt injection that reaches Claude could create new subagent definitions without any user interaction. Conversely, writes to `.git`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, `.husky`, and the rest of `.claude` still prompt in `bypassPermissions` — defying the mode's name.

> "Writes to `.git`, `.claude`, `.vscode`, `.idea`, and `.husky` directories still prompt for confirmation to prevent accidental corruption... Writes to `.claude/commands`, `.claude/agents`, and `.claude/skills` are exempt and do not prompt, because Claude routinely writes there when creating skills, subagents, and commands." [permissions §Permission modes](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#permission-modes)

#### SV-28. `Agent` allow rules dropped on auto mode entry — no warning (§9)

An orchestrator that pre-approved specific subagents with `Agent(MyAgent)` in `permissions.allow` finds those rules silently dropped the moment auto mode activates. Subagent invocations that were pre-approved now route through the classifier or may be blocked at spawn time. The rules return when auto mode is exited.

> "On entering auto mode, broad allow rules that grant arbitrary code execution are dropped: … `Agent` allow rules" [permission-modes §When auto mode falls back](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes#when-auto-mode-falls-back)

#### SV-29. `autoMode.soft_deny` or `autoMode.allow` replaces the entire default list (§9)

An author adds one custom safe pattern to `soft_deny`, expecting additive behavior. Instead, all built-in block rules are discarded. Force-push, data exfiltration, and `curl | bash` become silently permitted. The fix requires running `claude auto-mode defaults` first and copying the full default list before adding custom entries.

> "Setting `allow` or `soft_deny` replaces the entire default list for that section." [permissions §Override the block and allow r](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions#override-the-block-and-allow-rules)

#### SV-30. auto mode conversation-stated boundaries are lost on context compaction (§9)

An author tells Claude "don't push to production" at the start of a long session. The classifier enforces this by re-reading the transcript. After context compaction removes that message, the boundary evaporates — subsequent classifier checks do not see it. There is no notification that the boundary was lost. Hard guarantees require `permissions.deny` rules.

> "Boundaries are not stored as rules. The classifier re-reads them from the transcript on each check, so a boundary can be lost if context compaction removes the message that stated it. For a hard guarantee, add a deny rule instead." [permission-modes §Boundaries you state in conver](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permission-modes#boundaries-you-state-in-conversation)

#### SV-31. `allowUnixSockets` for Docker socket grants full host access (§9)

An author enables `allowUnixSockets` to let Claude run Docker commands inside the sandbox, expecting the sandbox to contain the blast radius. The Docker socket grants root-equivalent access to the host — the sandbox is effectively bypassed entirely.

> "if it is used to allow access to `/var/run/docker.sock` this would effectively grant access to the host system through exploiting the docker socket" [sandboxing §Security Limitations](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing#security-limitations)

#### SV-32. Background subagent `AskUserQuestion` calls fail silently — the subagent continues (§6, §8)

A background subagent whose `AskUserQuestion` call fails keeps going. The return value to the orchestrator is whatever the subagent produced *without* the answer — not an error, not a deferral. Authors expecting "subagent blocked, parent gets a clear failure" instead get "subagent finished with degraded output."

> "If a background subagent needs to ask clarifying questions, that tool call fails but the subagent continues." [sub-agents §Write subagent files](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#write-subagent-files)

#### SV-33. `model: inherit` (the default) silently produces provider-tier divergence (§5)

A subagent file with no `model:` field runs on different models for different users. A Max subscriber gets Opus 4.7; a Pro subscriber gets Sonnet 4.6; a Bedrock user gets Sonnet 4.5. An agent designed and tested by a Max user against Opus 4.7 may behave very differently when shipped to Pro or Bedrock users. The frontmatter looks identical in version control.

> "**Omitted**: If not specified, defaults to `inherit` (uses the same model as the main conversation)" [sub-agents §Write subagent files](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#write-subagent-files)

#### SV-34. Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry `opus` and `sonnet` aliases resolve to OLDER models than Anthropic API (§5)

Setting `model: opus` in subagent frontmatter does not get the same model across providers. On Anthropic API: Opus 4.7. On third-party providers: Opus 4.6 (a different model with different effort levels — Opus 4.6 lacks `xhigh`).

> "On the Anthropic API, `opus` resolves to Opus 4.7 and `sonnet` resolves to Sonnet 4.6. On Bedrock, Vertex, and Foundry, `opus` resolves to Opus 4.6 and `sonnet` resolves to Sonnet 4.5" [model-config §Model aliases](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config#model-aliases)

#### SV-35. `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_*_MODEL` silently reshapes every alias-using subagent (§5)

Setting `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL=claude-opus-4-6` reroutes every subagent with `model: opus` to Opus 4.6. The variable does not appear in the subagent resolution ladder; it changes what the alias means at resolution time. An author reading only the subagent ladder will not see this dependency.

> "use the following environment variables, which must be full **model names** (or equivalent for your API provider), to control the model names that the aliases map to." [model-config §Environment variables](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config#environment-variables)

#### SV-36. `availableModels` enumerates four enforcement vectors, omitting subagent frontmatter (§5)

An enterprise admin restricting `availableModels` to `["sonnet", "haiku"]` blocks Opus through `/model`, `--model`, the Config tool, and `ANTHROPIC_MODEL`. The source does not state whether a project subagent declaring `model: claude-opus-4-7` in frontmatter — or a session run with `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL=opus` — bypasses the restriction. An admin assuming `availableModels` is a complete enforcement perimeter may be wrong.

> "When `availableModels` is set, users cannot switch to models not in the list via `/model`, `--model` flag, Config tool, or `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` environment variable." [model-config §Restrict model selection](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config#restrict-model-selection)

#### SV-37. Custom `agents` path in `plugin.json` silently disables default `agents/` directory (§11)

A plugin author adds a single custom agent path expecting to extend the default — instead, every agent in `agents/` disappears silently. To keep the default and add more paths, list both explicitly: `"agents": ["./agents/", "./extras/"]`.

> "For `skills`, `commands`, `agents`, `outputStyles`, and `monitors`, a custom path replaces the default." [plugins-reference §Path behavior rules](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#path-behavior-rules)

#### SV-38. `strict: false` plus any `plugin.json` component declaration causes load failure (§11)

A plugin author who ships a `plugin.json` for metadata reasons (name, description, license) and also sets `strict: false` in the marketplace entry will find the plugin fails entirely, even if only one component field is declared in `plugin.json`.

> "If the plugin also has a `plugin.json` that declares components, that's a conflict and the plugin fails to load." [plugin-marketplaces §Strict mode](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces#strict-mode)

### Moderate

#### MD-1. `initialPrompt` is inert when the agent runs as a subagent (§1, §6)

`initialPrompt` is documented as effective "when this agent runs as the main session agent (via `--agent` or the `agent` setting)." Its behavior when the same definition is used as a subagent through the Agent tool is silent inert. An author who includes `initialPrompt` expecting per-invocation initialization gets nothing.

> "Auto-submitted as the first user turn when this agent runs as the main session agent (via `--agent` or the `agent` setting). Commands and skills are processed. Prepended to any user-provided prompt." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

#### MD-2. Manually added agent files do not load until session restart or `/agents` reload (§1, §2)

An author who drops a new `.claude/agents/my-agent.md` file into a running session and immediately tries to use the agent will find it does not exist. Other configuration files in Claude Code are loaded lazily; agent files are not.

> "Subagents are loaded at session start. If you create a subagent by manually adding a file, restart your session or use `/agents` to load it immediately." [sub-agents §Choose the subagent scope](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-the-subagent-scope)

#### MD-3. `--add-dir` directories are not scanned for agent definitions (§1, §2)

`--add-dir` grants Claude Code read access to an additional directory tree. An author who puts agent files in a shared library directory and adds it with `--add-dir` expecting those agents to be discovered will find they are not. Only the five priority locations are scanned. The exclusion is silent — no error, no warning.

> "Directories added with `--add-dir` grant file access only and are not scanned for subagents." [sub-agents §Understand automatic delegatio](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#understand-automatic-delegation)

#### MD-4. Higher-priority agent wins silently — lower-priority leaves no trace (§2)

An agent defined in `~/.claude/agents/` is completely hidden when a project-level `.claude/agents/` agent has the same name. No warning is emitted; the user agent simply does not appear. The only way to detect this is to run `claude agents` and inspect the "overridden" output.

> "When multiple subagents share the same name, the higher-priority location wins." [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

#### MD-5. `--plugin-dir` cannot override managed force-enabled plugins (§2, §11)

The general rule is that `--plugin-dir` beats the installed version. The managed exception breaks this mental model. A developer who uses `--plugin-dir` to test a local override of an org-managed plugin will silently continue running the managed version with no indication of why the local version did not take effect.

> "When a `--plugin-dir` plugin has the same name as an installed marketplace plugin, the local copy takes precedence for that session. ... Marketplace plugins force-enabled by managed settings are the only exception and cannot be overridden." [plugins §Test your plugins locally](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins#test-your-plugins-locally)

#### MD-6. "Use proactively" fires without a documented trigger condition (§3)

The phrase is presented as the mechanism for enabling proactive auto-invocation, but no source defines what event or condition causes the agent to fire proactively. An author who writes "Use proactively after code changes" cannot know whether the agent fires on every assistant turn, only when Claude detects a code-writing tool call, or only when the user explicitly requests code-related work.

> "To encourage proactive delegation, include phrases like 'use proactively' in your subagent's description field." [sub-agents §Invoke subagents explicitly](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#invoke-subagents-explicitly)

#### MD-7. Natural language invocation is advisory, not a guarantee (§3)

Authors who write `"Use the code-reviewer subagent to review my changes"` may expect this to be a directive. The docs explicitly classify it as advisory: Claude "decides whether to delegate." Only @-mention guarantees invocation for a specific task.

> "Natural language: name the subagent in your prompt; Claude decides whether to delegate" [sub-agents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)

#### MD-8. @-mention controls which agent runs, not what it receives (§3)

A user who writes `@"code-reviewer (agent)" check for SQL injection in auth.py` may expect the subagent receives exactly that instruction. Instead, Claude intercepts the full message and writes the subagent's task prompt itself. The subagent may receive a paraphrase, a reframing, or a summarized version.

> "The @-mention controls which subagent Claude invokes, not what prompt it receives." [sub-agents §Choose a model](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-a-model)

#### MD-9. Description routing does not apply when subagent definitions are used as agent-team teammates (§3)

An author who defines a subagent with a carefully crafted `description` for auto-routing may assume that `description` plays the same role when the agent is used as a teammate. It does not: teammates are spawned by explicit lead instruction, and the `description` field has no routing function in that context. Only `tools` and `model` carry over.

> "The teammate honors that definition's `tools` allowlist and `model`, and the definition's body is appended to the teammate's system prompt as additional instructions rather than replacing it." [agent-teams §Use subagent definitions for t](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams#use-subagent-definitions-for-teammates)

#### MD-10. `dontAsk` denies prompts but explicitly-allowed tools still work (§4)

The mode name suggests "don't bother me with questions." The actual behavior denies anything not pre-approved. An author setting `permissionMode: dontAsk` and listing `tools: Bash` without corresponding `permissions.allow` rules ends up with a subagent that can call Bash but gets denied at every invocation.

> "`dontAsk` | Auto-deny permission prompts (explicitly allowed tools still work)" [sub-agents §Choose a model](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-a-model)

#### MD-11. `EnterWorktree` and `ExitWorktree` are NEVER available to subagents (§4)

Listing `EnterWorktree` in a subagent's `tools:` field will not enable it. The platform-level exclusion is invisible from the frontmatter contract. To give a subagent worktree isolation, set `isolation: worktree` (a separate field).

> "`EnterWorktree` ... Not available to subagents" [tools-reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/tools-reference)

#### MD-12. `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` precedence over `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` is not stated (§5)

The subagent ladder cleanly establishes that `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` beats frontmatter `model:`. But the Phase A (main-conversation) ladder lists `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` above the settings `model` field. Whether `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` is consulted only as part of resolving "the main conversation's model" or whether it can short-circuit a subagent's explicit frontmatter `model:` value is not stated.

#### MD-13. `[1m]` suffix in pinning propagates to `opusplan` and is stripped pre-send (§5)

A pinned model ID like `claude-opus-4-7[1m]` has the `[1m]` removed before transit to the provider — but the 1M-context behavior is still activated. Authors inspecting actual API request bodies will not see the suffix and may conclude pinning failed.

> "The `[1m]` suffix applies the 1M context window to all usage of that alias, including `opusplan`. Claude Code strips the suffix before sending the model ID to your provider." [model-config §Pin models for third-party dep](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config#pin-models-for-third-party-deployments)

#### MD-14. `model: opusplan` may not be supported in subagent frontmatter (§5)

The Choose-a-model section in `sub-agents.md` enumerates only `sonnet`, `opus`, `haiku`. The full alias roster (`default`, `best`, `opusplan`, `sonnet[1m]`, `opus[1m]`) is documented elsewhere as accepted by `/model` and `--model`. Whether they work in subagent frontmatter is not stated.

> "**Model alias**: Use one of the available aliases: `sonnet`, `opus`, or `haiku`" [sub-agents §Built-in subagents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#built-in-subagents)

#### MD-15. Per-invocation `model` parameter is undocumented user-facing surface (§5, §6)

Rung 2 of the subagent ladder is reserved for a parameter Claude itself passes at invocation time. The docs do not specify whether users can influence this — for example, whether `--agents` JSON, settings, or any other mechanism can pin this value. The full Agent tool call wire format (other invocation-time overrides) is also undocumented.

> "When Claude invokes a subagent, it can also pass a `model` parameter for that specific invocation." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### MD-16. Built-in Explore is hardcoded to Haiku — `inherit` semantics do not apply (§5)

Explore and Plan are both built-in read-only research subagents, but Explore is pinned to Haiku while Plan inherits. A user upgrading to Opus 4.7 for the main conversation will get Opus reasoning in Plan-mode research but Haiku reasoning in Explore-routed research within the same session.

> Explore: "**Model**: Haiku (fast, low-latency)" / Plan: "**Model**: Inherits from main conversation" [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### MD-17. Haiku 3 retirement on April 19, 2026 (§5)

Any subagent frontmatter pinning `claude-3-haiku-20240307` will stop working on April 19, 2026. Frontmatter pins, unlike alias users, get no automatic migration.

> "Claude Haiku 3 (`claude-3-haiku-20240307`) is deprecated and will be retired on April 19, 2026. Migrate to [Claude Haiku 4.5](…) before the retirement date." [models-overview §Latest models comparison](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/models/overview#latest-models-comparison)

#### MD-18. Skills listed in subagent frontmatter inject FULL content at startup (§6, §7)

In the main conversation, skills load on demand. In a subagent, all listed skills inject at startup, consuming context immediately. Listing many large skills can exhaust subagent context before it performs any work.

> "The full content of each skill is injected into the subagent's context, not just made available for invocation." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

#### MD-19. CLAUDE.md loading in subagent context is ambiguous between CLI and SDK documentation (§6, §7)

The CLI source implies only the definition body and environment details arrive. The SDK source explicitly states `CLAUDE.md` loads into the subagent. Whether `CLAUDE.md` loads in a subagent invoked through the Claude Code CLI's Agent tool is not resolved by either source. Whether the user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` and `CLAUDE.local.md` follow the walk-up hierarchy in subagents is also unspecified.

#### MD-20. `.claude/rules/` inheritance into subagents is not specified (§7)

Path-scoped rules are a primary mechanism for organizing project instructions. Whether subagents apply them, or apply only the unscoped rules, or apply none, is not explicit in the primary sources.

#### MD-21. `settings.json` re-application inside subagent is not specified (§7)

settings.json holds permissions, hooks, environment variables, statusLine, model, outputStyle, and more. Documentation specifies inheritance for permission modes and frontmatter overrides for `hooks`, `mcpServers`, `permissionMode`, but does not state whether the subagent re-reads settings.json or whether `outputStyle` propagates.

#### MD-22. Inline MCP servers connect at start, disconnect at finish — no persistence (§7)

An MCP server with expensive startup (browser, database connection pool, model load) pays that cost on every subagent invocation. String references to already-configured servers reuse parent connections; inline definitions do not.

> "Inline servers defined here are connected when the subagent starts and disconnected when it finishes." [sub-agents §Manage subagent context](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#manage-subagent-context)

#### MD-23. Built-in Explore/Plan can only be disabled in non-interactive mode (§7)

The `CLAUDE_AGENT_SDK_DISABLE_BUILTIN_AGENTS` knob exists but is ineffective in interactive use. Users who want to suppress Explore/Plan delegation in interactive sessions must use `permissions.deny` with `Agent(Explore)` and `Agent(Plan)`.

> "Set to `1` to disable all built-in subagent types such as Explore and Plan. Only applies in non-interactive mode (the `-p` flag)." [env-vars](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/env-vars)

#### MD-24. Resumption ID given to orchestrator is unusable without an experimental flag (§8)

The orchestrator is told the agent ID on every subagent completion, suggesting it can address that agent later. In default Claude Code (without `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`) the only tool that uses that ID — `SendMessage` — is not present.

> "When a subagent completes, Claude receives its agent ID. Claude uses the `SendMessage` tool with the agent's ID as the `to` field to resume it. The `SendMessage` tool is only available when agent teams are enabled via `CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### MD-25. A subagent's auto-compaction can change what reaches the orchestrator without signal (§8)

A long subagent run can compact silently mid-task. The summary returned to the orchestrator is then synthesized over a post-compaction view of the subagent's own history. Whether the returned summary references compaction at all depends on the subagent's discretion.

> "Subagents support automatic compaction using the same logic as the main conversation. By default, auto-compaction triggers at approximately 95% capacity." [sub-agents §Choose the subagent scope](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-the-subagent-scope)

#### MD-26. Subagent authentication scope is undocumented (§9)

No source specifies whether a subagent inherits the parent session's credentials, initializes its own credential lookup from the environment, or operates entirely under the parent's auth context. An author deploying subagents in a cloud provider environment cannot determine from documentation alone whether the subagent will authenticate correctly.

#### MD-27. `once: true` silently has no effect in agent frontmatter (§10)

The `once` field is documented in the common fields table — fields that "apply to all hook types." The restriction to skills only appears as a parenthetical. An agent frontmatter author sets `once: true` on a `SubagentStart` hook expecting it to fire once per session; it fires on every spawn.

> "`once` — If `true`, runs only once per session then is removed. Skills only, not agents." [hooks](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)

#### MD-28. `agent_type` in common fields overrides `--agent` session value (§10)

A hook written to branch on `agent_type` for a named session (`--agent security-reviewer`) will see the spawned subagent's type (`Explore`, `Plan`, etc.) instead of the session type. A hook filtering on `agent_type == "security-reviewer"` will never match inside a subagent.

> "For subagents, the subagent's type takes precedence over the session's `--agent` value." [hooks §Common input fields](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#common-input-fields)

#### MD-29. `plugin.json` version silently wins over marketplace entry version (§11)

A marketplace maintainer bumps the version in `marketplace.json` expecting users to receive the update. If the plugin's `plugin.json` carries a different version, the marketplace value is ignored with no warning.

> "If also set in the marketplace entry, `plugin.json` takes priority. You only need to set it in one place." [plugins-reference §Metadata fields](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#metadata-fields)

#### MD-30. Relative plugin source paths fail when marketplace is added via URL (§11)

A marketplace author tests locally with `/plugin marketplace add ./my-marketplace` — relative paths work. They then host the raw `marketplace.json` at a URL and share that link. Every plugin with a relative `source` silently fails to install. The marketplace appears healthy (it loads); only individual plugin installs fail.

> "Relative paths only work when users add your marketplace via Git (GitHub, GitLab, or git URL). If users add your marketplace via a direct URL to the `marketplace.json` file, relative paths will not resolve correctly." [plugin-marketplaces §Relative paths](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-marketplaces#relative-paths)

#### MD-31. Version resolution requires a specific git tag convention; no tag means dependent plugin is disabled (§11)

A marketplace maintainer publishes a new plugin version by updating `plugin.json` and pushing. Without a `{plugin-name}--v{version}` tag on the commit, any downstream plugin that declares a version constraint becomes disabled. The plugin itself loads fine; only dependents break.

> "If no matching tag exists, the dependent plugin is disabled with an error listing the available versions." [plugin-dependencies §Tag plugin releases for versio](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugin-dependencies#tag-plugin-releases-for-version-resolution)

#### MD-32. `settings.json` `agent` key accepts a name that may silently not match (§11)

`settings.json` ignores unknown keys. If the `agent` value does not match any agent file in `agents/`, no error is emitted and no agent is activated — Claude Code falls back to default behavior.

> "Settings from `settings.json` take priority over `settings` declared in `plugin.json`. Unknown keys are silently ignored." [plugins §Ship default settings with you](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins#ship-default-settings-with-your-plugin)

### Minor

#### MN-1. `Task(...)` is a backward-compatibility alias for `Agent(...)` (§4)

Documentation written before v2.1.63 used `Task(...)`. Mixed codebases may have both forms in different files. There is no version-gated warning; the alias is silent. Authors searching for `Task` in tool lists may miss `Agent` entries and vice versa.

> "In version 2.1.63, the Task tool was renamed to Agent. Existing `Task(...)` references in settings and agent definitions still work as aliases." [sub-agents §Control subagent capabilities](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities)

#### MN-2. `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` is deprecated but not stated to be ignored (§5)

"Deprecated" does not mean "ignored." The variable is still listed and described. An environment with both `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` and `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL` set has undocumented precedence.

> "Note: `ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL` is deprecated in favor of `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL`." [model-config §Environment variables](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/model-config#environment-variables)

#### MN-3. Project-agent cwd walk termination is undocumented (§2)

The docs describe the walk but specify no stopping condition. Authors who run Claude Code from a deeply nested subdirectory of a monorepo may pick up `.claude/agents/` definitions from an ancestor they did not intend to include.

> "Project subagents are discovered by walking up from the current working directory." [sub-agents §Choose the subagent scope](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#choose-the-subagent-scope)

#### MN-4. Disabling a plugin mid-session does not stop running monitors (§11)

An operator disables a plugin expecting all its background processes to stop. Monitors started by that plugin continue running and delivering notifications for the remainder of the session. They stop only at session end. (Affects agents indirectly when an agent activates monitors.)

> "Disabling a plugin mid-session does not stop monitors that are already running. They stop when the session ends." [plugins-reference §Monitors](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/plugins-reference#monitors)


---

## Appendix B — Where the platform docs are silent

Consolidated list of every "primary sources do not specify" item, grouped by section.

### §1 — Frontmatter

- `maxTurns` platform default when the field is omitted.
- `color` effect in non-interactive or headless sessions.
- Worktree persistence duration after the agent makes changes (`isolation: worktree` cleanup is documented only when no changes are made).
- `memory` interaction with explicit `disallowedTools: Write, Edit` exclusions.
- `effort` values per model — docs state "available levels depend on the model" without enumerating the per-model matrix.
- `name` field character constraints beyond "lowercase letters and hyphens" (digits permitted? leading character? maximum length?).
- Order of `disallowedTools` and memory tool injection — whether memory tool injection occurs before or after `disallowedTools` is applied.

### §2 — Discovery

- Whether the directory walk for project agents stops at a git root or filesystem root.
- Exact file extension requirement (all examples use `.md` but no source explicitly states `.md` is required).
- Behavior when two plugin agents within the same plugin share the same `name` field.
- Whether `--agents` CLI agents follow the same precedence as managed-settings-deployed agents.
- Maximum number of agents discoverable or active at once.
- Discovery behavior when `plugin.json` specifies a custom `agents` path that does not exist.
- Whether `/reload-plugins` re-runs the project directory walk or only reloads plugin-sourced agents.
- Auto-update behavior for already-loaded agent definitions inside a running session.

### §3 — Description-trigger semantics

- Matching algorithm — embedding distance, token overlap, or model judgment is not stated.
- "Use proactively" threshold conditions, frequency, or trigger event.
- Multi-subagent conflict resolution when two descriptions overlap at the same scope level.
- Whether description length, structure, or style affects routing quality.
- Negative description patterns (whether descriptions can specify when *not* to invoke).
- How the dispatcher weights its three inputs (request, description, context) relative to each other.

### §4 — Tool restriction

- Whether MCP tool names appear as `mcp__server__tool` strings inside `tools` / `disallowedTools` frontmatter (the syntax is documented for permission rules and hook matchers but no verbatim example demonstrates it in subagent frontmatter).
- Whether `tools: mcp__server__*` wildcard matching works inside the frontmatter `tools` field.
- Order of operations between `disallowedTools` and `Agent(...)` allowlist semantics when both interact.
- What happens when `tools` lists a tool that does not exist (typo, removed tool, version-gated tool).
- Whether `tools` field is whitespace-sensitive between commas.
- Whether built-in subagent tool restrictions (Explore, Plan as "denied access to Write and Edit tools") can be overridden by user permission rules.
- Whether `disallowedTools` accepts `Agent(...)` syntax in agent frontmatter.
- Whether the `Skill` tool's permission requirement overrides per-skill `allowed-tools` frontmatter restrictions.

### §5 — Model selection

- Whether `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` overrides a subagent's frontmatter `model:` field.
- Whether `availableModels` filters subagent frontmatter `model:` values or `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL`.
- Whether aliases other than `sonnet`/`opus`/`haiku` (e.g., `default`, `best`, `opusplan`, `sonnet[1m]`, `opus[1m]`) are accepted in the subagent `model:` frontmatter field.
- Whether `ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_MODEL_OPTION` model IDs are accepted as subagent frontmatter `model:` values.
- Whether the per-invocation `model` parameter is exposed to user configuration.
- Whether `CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL` accepts aliases, full model IDs, or both.
- Whether `model: inherit` re-resolves on `/model` mid-session.
- Whether the `--agents` JSON `model` field accepts the same value set as file-based frontmatter.

### §6 — Invocation lifecycle

- Exact Agent tool call wire format (precise JSON shape, full parameter list).
- What "basic environment details" includes beyond working directory.
- Skill content injection order when multiple skills are listed.
- Timeout behavior independent of `maxTurns`.
- What the subagent sees if `initialPrompt` is set and it is invoked as a subagent (not as `--agent`).
- Whether the parent's `CLAUDE.md` loads into the subagent context — direct tension between CLI source (silent) and SDK source (states it does load).

### §7 — Context isolation

- Whether the user-level CLAUDE.md (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`) loads for an Agent-tool-invoked subagent.
- Whether `CLAUDE.local.md` loads for a subagent.
- Whether `claudeMdExcludes` applies inside the subagent's context.
- Whether `.claude/rules/` files (with or without `paths:` frontmatter) load for a subagent.
- Whether `settings.json` permissions, hooks, environment variables, and other keys re-apply inside the subagent.
- General-OS environment variable propagation through the Agent tool boundary.
- Whether parent CLAUDE.md content is included as text inside the subagent's system prompt or loaded through the same user-message channel as in the main session.
- Whether nested CLAUDE.md files in subdirectories load on demand inside the subagent.
- Whether the parent's `agent` setting interacts with Agent-tool-invoked subagents.
- Whether the parent's `outputStyle` propagates to the subagent.
- Whether `--append-system-prompt` content propagates to the subagent.

### §8 — Output routing

- No documented, named return-value JSON schema for the Agent tool.
- No `SubagentStopFailure` event documented.
- No token-usage telemetry on return.
- No explicit ordering of `SubagentStop` vs `WorktreeRemove`.
- Behavior on user interrupt of a subagent.
- Behavior of `Ctrl+B` backgrounding mid-flight on the return-path payload.
- Truncation policy for the returned summary.
- Failure surface from a `TaskCompleted` block on a subagent's task.

### §9 — Permissions, sandbox, IAM

- Subagent credential inheritance — whether a subagent reuses the parent session's credentials or authenticates independently.
- `permissionMode` in subagent frontmatter outside auto mode — sources state this field is ignored when auto mode is active; behavior when auto mode is NOT active is not documented.
- Per-subagent permission rule isolation.
- `Agent` allow rules behavior in non-auto mode (default, acceptEdits, plan, dontAsk).
- Sandbox interaction with subagent spawning — whether the Agent tool invocation itself is sandboxed.
- Multiple credential types for subagents using cloud providers.
- `apiKeyHelper` TTL and refresh during long subagent runs.

### §10 — Hook events

- Whether `SubagentStart` fires for agent-hook-spawned subagents.
- Whether hooks inside a subagent see the subagent's `permission_mode`.
- Maximum nesting depth for subagent hooks.
- What happens to SubagentStop output when the subagent runs inside an agent-based hook.
- `WorktreeRemove` timing relative to `SubagentStop`.
- How `additionalContext` from `SubagentStart` is scoped (system reminder, user message, other injection).

### §11 — Plugin packaging

- Whether `plugin.json` supports a `settings` key inline (distinct from the `settings.json` file at the plugin root).
- Maximum number of agents per plugin.
- Whether agent `name` frontmatter is required or optional within a plugin (filename-fallback behavior is documented for skills, not agents).
- Behavior when `agents` array in `plugin.json` specifies a directory vs. a specific file for resolution.
- Whether marketplace entry `agents` field replaces or merges with `plugin.json` `agents` field when `strict: true`.
- Minimum Claude Code version required for plugin agents specifically.

### Cross-domain notes

- The `sub-agents.md` page is canonical for subagent contracts and is cited by every section.
- The `permissions.md` page covers the rule syntax referenced from §4 and §9.
- The `hooks.md` page is the authority for §10 and the return-path section of §8.
- The `plugins-reference.md` and `plugins.md` pages cover §11 and the plugin-restriction parts of §1, §2, §6.
- The `model-config.md` and `models-overview.md` pages anchor §5.
- The `context-window.md`, `claude-directory.md`, `memory.md`, and `env-vars.md` pages anchor §7.
- SDK pages (`agent-sdk-subagents.md`, `agent-sdk-agent-loop.md`) are cited only where the CLI sources are silent or to surface a CLI/SDK tension; SDK behavior is out of scope for this manual.
